Title: Do not fetch sensitive information from a GET request.
Description: Do not fetch sensitive information from a GET request.
Remediation: It looks like you are using query parameters to fetch sensitive information. Placing sensitive data in a GET request increases the risk of the data being exposed to an attacker. It is advised to read such data from a POST request body.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/598.html)

Title: CWE-338,330,1241 - Pseudorandom number generators
Description: Using pseudorandom number generators (PRNGs) is security-sensitive. It might be possible to guess the next value generated by PRNGs and use this guess to impersonate another user or access sensitive information.
Remediation: We detected the use of pseudorandom number generators (PRNGs) or hardcoded values with encryption algorithms. In these cases, it is possible to guess the next value and use this guess to impersonate another user or to access sensitive information. [Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/338.html)

Title: CWE-352,1275 - Cross-site request forgery
Description: Insecure configuration can lead to a cross-site request forgery (CSRF) vulnerability. This can enable an attacker to trick end users into performing unwanted actions while authenticated.
Remediation: Disabling cross-site request forgery (CSRF) protection can make your users vulnerable to attacks. Enable CSRF if your application can be accessed through a browser and another token mechanism isn't in place.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/352.html)

Title: CWE-352,1275 - Cross-site request forgery
Description: Insecure configuration can lead to a cross-site request forgery (CSRF) vulnerability. This can enable an attacker to trick end users into performing unwanted actions while authenticated.
Remediation: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) protection is missing for state-changing operations. This vulnerability allows attackers to trick authenticated users into unknowingly performing unintended actions (e.g., changing passwords, transferring funds, or modifying data) by submitting malicious requests on their behalf. Implement anti-CSRF tokens for all POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE operations, or use SameSite cookies with 'strict' or 'lax' settings as an alternative defense. Reference: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Cross-Site_Request_Forgery_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html

Title: CWE-117,93 - Log injection
Description: User-provided inputs must be sanitized before they are logged. An attacker can use unsanitized input to break a log's integrity, forge log entries, or bypass log monitors.
Remediation: Potential log injection detected. Ensure all untrusted input is properly sanitized before logging. Use parameterized logging or validate input against a allow list to prevent log injection vulnerabilities. Consider using a dedicated logging library's built-in sanitization features when available. Learn more - https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/117.html

Title: CWE-22,23 - Path traversal
Description: Creating file paths from untrusted input could allow a malicious actor to access arbitrary files on a disk by manipulating the file name in the path.
Remediation: Path traversal vulnerability detected. An attacker could access files outside the intended directory, potentially exposing sensitive information or executing malicious code. Validate and sanitize file paths by using path.normalize() and checking that the resolved path is within the expected directory. Learn More - https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/22.html.

Title: CWE-94 - Unsanitized input is run as code
Description: Running scripts generated from unsanitized inputs (for example, evaluating expressions that include user-provided strings) can lead to malicious behavior and inadvertently running code remotely.
Remediation: Untrusted input is passed to a method that executes arbitrary code, creating a code injection vulnerability. This allows attackers to execute malicious code within the application, potentially leading to data theft or system compromise. Validate input using allowlists with `Arrays.asList().contains()`, use regex matching with `String.matches()`, or parse input safely with `Integer.parseInt()` or `Boolean.parseBoolean()`. Remediation: It appears that you are using the `unserialize()` or `load` function for `js-yaml` on tainted input. This happens when users directly deserialize unvalidated malicious or hostile data that attackers send to the in-memory structure. Title: CWE-918 - Server-side request forgery
Description: Insufficient sanitization of potentially untrusted URLs on the server side can lead to the server issuing requests to unwanted hosts, ports, or protocols, which can bypass proxies, firewalls, and other security measures.
Remediation: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) vulnerability detected.  Untrusted user input is being used in a network request (in fetch, axios, or http.request)  without proper validation. This can allow attackers to craft requests to internal systems, access metadata services,  or perform internal port scans. To fix this issue, strictly validate and sanitize the input URL  using a library like `node:url` for parsing, enforce an allowlist of trusted domains,  and block access to private IP ranges (e.g., 127.0.0.1, 169.254.169.254).  Learn more: https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/Server_Side_Request_Forgery

Title: CWE-329,1204,323 - Weak Initialization Vector (IV)
Description: Using a static or predictable initialization vector (IV) for a cryptographic cipher can make the resulting ciphertext much more predictable and susceptible to dictionary attacks.
Remediation: Using insecure random number generators can lead to predictable random values. This predictability can be exploited by attackers to guess sensitive values or break cryptographic operations. To fix this, use `new SecureRandom()` to instantiate a `java.security.SecureRandom` object instead of using non-cryptographic random number generators (like java.util.Random, Math.random(), or Apache Commons random utilities). For more information about secure random number generation, see: https://owasp.org/www-community/vulnerabilities/Insecure_Randomness

Title: CWE-703 - Improper error handling

Title: CWE-20,79,80 - Cross-site scripting
Description: User-controllable input must be sanitized before it's included in output used to dynamically generate a web page. Unsanitized user input can introduce cross-side scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities that can lead to inadvertedly running malicious code in a trusted context.
Remediation: Untrusted data must be properly encoded or sanitized before beingincorporated into web page content or used to generate dynamic output.Failure to do so can result in Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities,enabling attackers to inject malicious scripts into the application. Thesescripts, when executed in users' browsers, can lead to various securitybreaches such as session hijacking, data theft, or unauthorized actionsperformed within the victim's session context. To mitigate this risk,use built-in template escaping mechanisms, HTML escape functions like`html.escape()`, or dedicated security libraries such as Bleach.Learn more: https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/xss/

Title: CWE-79 - Reflected cross site scripting
Description: Rule for detecting reflected cross-site scripting in code that uses the Spring or Horizonte framework.
Remediation: A cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability was detected in your code, allowing an attacker to run arbitrary JavaScript in users' browser. The attacker could steal session cookies, download and install malicious files, redirect users to malicious domains, and more.Always assume all inputs are malicious, such as user-provided parameters, hidden fields, cookies, headers, and the URL. Title: CWE-400,664 - Resource leak
Description: Allocated resources are not released properly. This can slow down or crash your system. They must be closed along all paths to prevent a resource leak.

Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule
Description: Resources that implement AutoCloseable or require explicit closure must be properly managed throughout their lifecycle. The recommended approach is to use try-with-resources block which ensures automatic and safe resource closure, regardless of whether an exception occurs or not. Failing to properly close resources can lead to resource leaks, memory issues, and system instability. This applies to both application resources and testing components.
Remediation: The code is not properly closing resources, which can lead to resource leaks. Unclosed resources may cause memory leaks or other system resource issues, potentially degrading application performance over time. If an exception occurs before the explicit close() call, the resource will not be closed, increasing the risk of leaks. To remediate this, use the try-with-resources statement to automatically close AutoCloseable resources.Alternatively, if try-with-resources cannot be used, ensure that close() is called in a finally block to guarantee proper resource cleanup even when exceptions occur.[Learn more](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/essential/exceptions/tryResourceClose.html)

Title: CWE-798 - Hardcoded credentials
Description: Hardcoded credentials can be intercepted by malicious actors. Even after removing them from the code they may still pose a risk because an attacker might have recorded them to use them at a later point in time.
Remediation: We detected a hardcoded database connection password in your code. This could allow anyone with access to your server to perform privileged actions on the database. We recommend that you avoid hardcoded passwords when connecting to a database and instead use environment variables or a configuration file to set the password.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/259.html)

Title: CWE-208 - Timing attack
Description: Operators like `==` and `===` are not time-safe and can make your application vulnerable to a timing attack, which might enable attackers to infer security-sensitive information.
Remediation: Avoid using `==` and `===` operators with sensitive data fields like passwords, api keys, tokens etc. as they are vulnerable to timing attack. Consider using `crypto.timingSafeEqual` or `secure-compare` for time-safe string comparison.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/208.html)

Title: CWE-79,80 - Cross-site scripting
Description: User-controllable input must be sanitized before it's included in output used to dynamically generate a web page. Unsanitized user input can introduce cross-side scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities that can lead to inadvertedly running malicious code in a trusted context.
Remediation: A potential Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability has been identified, where untrusted input is being reflected in the response without proper sanitization. This can allow an attacker to inject and execute malicious scripts in the user's browser, potentially compromising sensitive data or application behavior. To mitigate this risk, it is strongly recommended to sanitize or encode all user input before rendering it in the DOM or returning it in a response. Commonly used methods include DOMPurify.sanitize(), encodeURIComponent(), escape(), etc., depending on the use case and framework in use. Learn more - https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/79.html

Title: CWE-400 - Denial of service rule

Title: CWE-276 - Incorrect default Permissions
Description: Incorrect default permissions in JavaScript code can lead to serious security issues, including unauthorized access, data exposure, or modification of sensitive files. Proper setting of file and directory permissions, along with restrictive defaults (e.g., 0o600 for files or 0o700 for directories), is essential to ensure that only authorized users have access. Explicit permission management mitigates risks associated with relying on system defaults, which may be too permissive.
Remediation: Insecure file permissions (0o777, 0o666, 0o755, 0o644) detected. These can allow unauthorized users to read, write, or execute the file, leading to data breaches or privilege escalation. Use more restrictive permissions such as 0o600 (read-write for owner only) to ensure proper security. For more details on secure file permissions, refer to [CWE-276](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/276.html).

Title: CWE-489 - Enabling and overriding debug feature
Description: Don't enable or override an application's debug feature. Instead, use OS environment variables to set up the debug feature.
Remediation: Enabling an application's debug or test feature makes it easier for developers to find bugs, but it also gives attackers access to detailed information about both the system running the application and users. To make your code secure, do not enable the debug or test feature on production servers. For more information, see [CWE-489](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/489.html).

Title: CWE-352,1275 - Cross-site request forgery
Description: Insecure configuration can lead to a cross-site request forgery (CSRF) vulnerability. This can enable an attacker to trick end users into performing unwanted actions while authenticated.
Remediation: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) protection is missing for state-changing operations. This vulnerability allows attackers to trick authenticated users into unknowingly performing unintended actions (e.g., changing passwords, transferring funds, or modifying data) by submitting malicious requests on their behalf. Implement anti-CSRF tokens for all POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE operations, or use SameSite cookies with 'strict' or 'lax' settings as an alternative defense. Reference: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Cross-Site_Request_Forgery_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html

Title: CWE-352,1275 - Cross-site request forgery
Description: Insecure configuration can lead to a cross-site request forgery (CSRF) vulnerability. This can enable an attacker to trick end users into performing unwanted actions while authenticated.
Remediation: Disabling cross-site request forgery (CSRF) protection can make your users vulnerable to attacks. Enable CSRF if your application can be accessed through a browser and another token mechanism isn't in place.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/352.html)

Title: CWE-22 - Path traversal
Description: Constructing path names with unsanitized user input can lead to path traversal attacks (for example, `../../..`) that allow an attacker access to file system resources.
Remediation: You are using potentially untrusted inputs to access a file path. To protect your code from a path traversal attack, verify that your inputs are sanitized. Learn more about path traversal vulnerabilities on the [Common Weakness Enumeration](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/22.html) website and the [Open Web Application Security Project](https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/Path_Traversal) website.

Title: CWE-117,93 - Log injection
Description: User-provided inputs must be sanitized before they are logged. An attacker can use unsanitized input to break a log's integrity, forge log entries, or bypass log monitors.
Remediation: Potential log injection detected. Ensure all untrusted input is properly sanitized before logging. Use parameterized logging or validate input against a allow list to prevent log injection vulnerabilities. Consider using a dedicated logging library's built-in sanitization features when available. Learn more - https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/117.html

Title: CWE-379,377 - Insecure temporary file or directory
Description: Insecure ways of creating temporary files and directories can lead to race conditions and other security vulnerabilities such as privilege escalation. Race conditions can be exploited for denial of service attacks.
Remediation: It looks like you're creating or opening a file in a global directory. Manipulating files from these directories can expose your application to race conditions. Also, an attacker can access, modify, corrupt, or delete files from these directories. To prevent this, use a dedicated subdirectory with controlled permissions or secure-by-design APIs to create temporary files. For more information, see [CWE-379](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/379.html).

Title: CWE-400 - Denial of service rule
Description: A denial of service (DOS) attack can make it impossible for legitimate users to access computer systems, network, services or other resources.
Remediation: An attacker can provide an input stream or file that contains an excessive number of lines without a terminating condition. The call to `readLine()` might allow an attacker to crash the program or otherwise make it unavailable to legitimate users which causes denial of service. To avoid this, make sure you either enforce a limit on the lines read or apply proper input sanitization.

Title: CWE-327,328 - Weak algorithm used for Password Hashing
Description: Weak algorithm used for Password Hashing. Consider using stronger algorithms, such as Argon2, PBKDF2, or scrypt.
Remediation: To improve your code's security, use a password hashing function, such as the key derivation and stretching algorithm, the [PHC](https://password-hashing.net/) or [Argon2](https://password-hashing.net/#argon2), with the correct time and memory cost parameters. Avoid using the less secure `BLAKE2`, `MD5`, `SHA-1`, `SHA-256` and `SHA-3` hash functions for user passwords.  [CWE 916](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/916.html).[Learn more](https://docs.python.org/3/library/hashlib.html#key-derivation)
Detector Name: Weak algorithm used for Password Hashing

Title: CWE-20,79,80 - Cross-site scripting
Description: User-controllable input must be sanitized before it's included in output used to dynamically generate a web page. Unsanitized user input can introduce cross-side scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities that can lead to inadvertedly running malicious code in a trusted context.
Remediation: Untrusted data must be properly encoded or sanitized before beingincorporated into web page content or used to generate dynamic output.Failure to do so can result in Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities,enabling attackers to inject malicious scripts into the application. Thesescripts, when executed in users' browsers, can lead to various securitybreaches such as session hijacking, data theft, or unauthorized actionsperformed within the victim's session context. To mitigate this risk,use built-in template escaping mechanisms, HTML escape functions like`html.escape()`, or dedicated security libraries such as Bleach.Learn more: https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/xss/

Title: CWE-798 - Hardcoded credentials
Description: Hardcoded credentials can be intercepted by malicious actors. Even after removing them from the code they may still pose a risk because an attacker might have recorded them to use them at a later point in time.
Remediation: It appears your code contains a hardcoded password. This poses a serious security risk as it may allow unauthorized access to sensitive data or systems. To remediate this issue, it is recommended to use AWS Secrets Manager to store and access secrets:	a) Visit Secrets Manager on the AWS Management Console.	b) Choose the secret type on the console page and follow the instructions.	c) Use the suggested code samples to retrieve the secret.To learn more about password management best practices, see [OWASP password storage cheat sheet](https://www.owasp.org/index.php/Password_Storage_Cheat_Sheet).

Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule
Description: Resources that implement AutoCloseable or require explicit closure must be properly managed throughout their lifecycle. The recommended approach is to use try-with-resources block which ensures automatic and safe resource closure, regardless of whether an exception occurs or not. Failing to properly close resources can lead to resource leaks, memory issues, and system instability. This applies to both application resources and testing components.
Remediation: The code is not properly closing resources, which can lead to resource leaks. Unclosed resources may cause memory leaks or other system resource issues, potentially degrading application performance over time. If an exception occurs before the explicit close() call, the resource will not be closed, increasing the risk of leaks. To remediate this, use the try-with-resources statement to automatically close AutoCloseable resources.Alternatively, if try-with-resources cannot be used, ensure that close() is called in a finally block to guarantee proper resource cleanup even when exceptions occur.[Learn more](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/essential/exceptions/tryResourceClose.html)

Title: CWE-502,1321 - Deserialization of untrusted object
Description: Deserialization of untrusted or potentially malformed data can be exploited for denial of service or to induce running untrusted code.
Remediation: It appears that you are using `JSON.parse()` on tainted user input. This could lead to data compromise, acccess control attacks, prototype pollution,  denial of service (DoS) attacks, or authentication bypass. To increase the security of your code,  do not parse tainted user input.

Title: CWE-704 - Incorrect type conversion or cast
Description: The software does not correctly convert an object, resource, or structure from one type to a different type. This could expose the application to security vulnerabilities like NaN injection.
Remediation: We observed that you are using `Integer.toHexString` for `StringBuilder.append()` which weakens computation of the hash value since it introduces more collisions. Instead of that you can use `String.format`.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/704.html)

Title: CWE-208 - Timing attack
Description: Operators like `==` and `===` are not time-safe and can make your application vulnerable to a timing attack, which might enable attackers to infer security-sensitive information.
Remediation: Avoid using `==` and `===` operators with sensitive data fields like passwords, api keys, tokens etc. as they are vulnerable to timing attack. Consider using `crypto.timingSafeEqual` or `secure-compare` for time-safe string comparison.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/208.html)

Title: CWE-918 - Server-side request forgery
Description: Insufficient sanitization of potentially untrusted URLs on the server side can lead to the server issuing requests to unwanted hosts, ports, or protocols, which can bypass proxies, firewalls, and other security measures.
Remediation: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) vulnerability detected.  Untrusted user input is being used in a network request (in fetch, axios, or http.request)  without proper validation. This can allow attackers to craft requests to internal systems, access metadata services,  or perform internal port scans. To fix this issue, strictly validate and sanitize the input URL  using a library like `node:url` for parsing, enforce an allowlist of trusted domains,  and block access to private IP ranges (e.g., 127.0.0.1, 169.254.169.254).  Learn more: https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/Server_Side_Request_Forgery

Title: CWE-614 - Insecure cookie

To increase the security of your code, set the `Secure` flag to `True`.  [Learn more](https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Session_Management_Cheat_Sheet.html#secure-attribute)

Title: CWE-434 - Unrestricted upload of dangerous file type

Title: Do not fetch sensitive information from a GET request.

Title: CWE-798 - Hardcoded credentials

Title: CWE-22,23 - Path traversal
Description: Creating file paths from untrusted input could allow a malicious actor to access arbitrary files on a disk by manipulating the file name in the path.
Remediation: Path traversal vulnerability detected. An attacker could access files outside the intended directory, potentially exposing sensitive information or executing malicious code. Validate and sanitize file paths by using path.normalize() and checking that the resolved path is within the expected directory. Learn More - https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/22.html.

Title: CWE-352,1275 - Cross-site request forgery
Description: Insecure configuration can lead to a cross-site request forgery (CSRF) vulnerability. This can enable an attacker to trick end users into performing unwanted actions while authenticated.
Remediation: Disabling cross-site request forgery (CSRF) protection can make your users vulnerable to attacks. Enable CSRF if your application can be accessed through a browser and another token mechanism isn't in place.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/352.html)

Title: CWE-352,1275 - Cross-site request forgery
Description: Insecure configuration can lead to a cross-site request forgery (CSRF) vulnerability. This can enable an attacker to trick end users into performing unwanted actions while authenticated.
Remediation: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) protection is missing for state-changing operations. This vulnerability allows attackers to trick authenticated users into unknowingly performing unintended actions (e.g., changing passwords, transferring funds, or modifying data) by submitting malicious requests on their behalf. Implement anti-CSRF tokens for all POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE operations, or use SameSite cookies with 'strict' or 'lax' settings as an alternative defense. Reference: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Cross-Site_Request_Forgery_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html

Title: CWE-117,93 - Log injection

Remediation: Potential log injection detected. Ensure all untrusted input is properly sanitized before logging. Learn more - https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/117.html

Title: CWE-329,1204,323 - Weak Initialization Vector (IV)
Description: Using a static or predictable initialization vector (IV) for a cryptographic cipher can make the resulting ciphertext much more predictable and susceptible to dictionary attacks.
Remediation: Using insecure random number generators can lead to predictable random values. This predictability can be exploited by attackers to guess sensitive values or break cryptographic operations. To fix this, use `new SecureRandom()` to instantiate a `java.security.SecureRandom` object instead of using non-cryptographic random number generators (like java.util.Random, Math.random(), or Apache Commons random utilities). For more information about secure random number generation, see: https://owasp.org/www-community/vulnerabilities/Insecure_Randomness

Title: CWE-502 - Deserialization of untrusted object
Description: Deserialization of untrusted or potentially malformed data can be exploited for denial of service or to induce running untrusted code.
Remediation: It looks like you are constructing an unsafe instance of `Yaml`, which might allow attackers to execute arbitrary code on the server, abuse application logic or lead to denial of service attacks. We recommend you to use a `SafeConstructor` when constructing a new `Yaml`.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/502.html)

Title: CWE-117,93 - Log injection
Description: User-provided inputs must be sanitized before they are logged. An attacker can use unsanitized input to break a log's integrity, forge log entries, or bypass log monitors.
Remediation: You have a log statement that might use the unsanitized input read through `request`. Depending on the context, this could result in:1. A log injection attack that breaks log integrity, forges log entries, or bypasses monitors that use the logs. This can also result in remote code execution for certain versions of the Log4j library. To mitigate the risk, make sure you are using Log4j version higher than 2.16. It is a good practice to also ensure proper input validation before writing to logs and use strict output encoding, especially when encoding `CRLF` characters. Possible options are: Replace `` and `` in the logged message; add an [encoding](https://logging.apache.org/log4j/2.x/manual/layouts.html#PatternLayout), such as `%enc{%m}{CRLF}` to the pattern layout of your logger; or use an `Appender` with a `JsonTemplateLayout`. [Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/117.html)2. A sensitive information leak that exposes users' credentials, private information, or identifying information to an attacker. To preserve privacy in your code, redact sensitive user information before logging it. [Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/532.html)

Title: CWE-400,664 - Resource leak
Description: Allocated resources are not released properly. This can slow down or crash your system. They must be closed along all paths to prevent a resource leak.
Remediation: **Problem**This line of code contains a resource that might not be closed properly. This can cause a resource leak that slows down or crashes your system.**Fix**Consider closing the following resource: *conn*. The resource is allocated by call *sqlite3.connect*. The resource is referenced at line 56. The resource is returned at line 57. There are other execution paths that don't close the resource or return it, for example, when *Connection.execute()* throws an exception. To prevent this resource leak, close *conn* along these other paths before you exit this method.**More info**[View details about the `with` statement in the Python developer's guide](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0343/) (external link).

Title: CWE-770,400 - Limit request length

We recommend that you set the `fileSize` <= 8mb. [Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/400.html)

Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule
Description: Resources that implement AutoCloseable or require explicit closure must be properly managed throughout their lifecycle. The recommended approach is to use try-with-resources block which ensures automatic and safe resource closure, regardless of whether an exception occurs or not. Failing to properly close resources can lead to resource leaks, memory issues, and system instability. This applies to both application resources and testing components.
Remediation: The code is not properly closing resources, which can lead to resource leaks. Unclosed resources may cause memory leaks or other system resource issues, potentially degrading application performance over time. If an exception occurs before the explicit close() call, the resource will not be closed, increasing the risk of leaks. To remediate this, use the try-with-resources statement to automatically close AutoCloseable resources.Alternatively, if try-with-resources cannot be used, ensure that close() is called in a finally block to guarantee proper resource cleanup even when exceptions occur.[Learn more](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/essential/exceptions/tryResourceClose.html)

This can allow attackers to craft requests to internal systems, access metadata services,  or perform internal port scans. To fix this issue, strictly validate and sanitize the input URL  using a library like `node:url` for parsing, enforce an allowlist of trusted domains,  and block access to private IP ranges (e.g., 127.0.0.1, 169.254.169.254).  Learn more: https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/Server_Side_Request_Forgery

Title: CWE-306 - Missing Authentication for Critical Function
Description: When authentication checks are not applied, users are able to access data or perform actions that they should not be allowed to access or perform. The lack of authentication checks can cause the exposure of information, denial of service, and arbitrary code execution. We recommend that you apply authentication checks to all access points.
Remediation: This route is missing an authentication check, which may allow unauthorized access to sensitive functionality. Ensure that authentication middleware or mechanisms are applied to protect critical routes. Failing to do so can lead to security vulnerabilities such as unauthorized access. Learn more: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Authentication_Cheat_Sheet.html

Title: CWE-614 - Insecure cookie
Description: Insecure cookie settings can lead to unencrypted cookie transmission. Even if a cookie doesn't contain sensitive data now, sensitive data could be added later. It's good practice to transmit all cookies only through secure channels.
Remediation: We detected the use of insecure cookies. Insecure cookie vulnerabilities might leak session IDs and other sensitive information. To increase the security of your code, set the `Secure` flag to `True`.  [Learn more](https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Session_Management_Cheat_Sheet.html#secure-attribute)

Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule
Description: Resources that implement AutoCloseable or require explicit closure must be properly managed throughout their lifecycle. The recommended approach is to use try-with-resources block which ensures automatic and safe resource closure, regardless of whether an exception occurs or not. Failing to properly close resources can lead to resource leaks, memory issues, and system instability. This applies to both application resources and testing components.
Remediation: The code is not properly closing resources, which can lead to resource leaks. Unclosed resources may cause memory leaks or other system resource issues, potentially degrading application performance over time. If an exception occurs before the explicit close() call, the resource will not be closed, increasing the risk of leaks. To remediate this, use the try-with-resources statement to automatically close AutoCloseable resources.Alternatively, if try-with-resources cannot be used, ensure that close() is called in a finally block to guarantee proper resource cleanup even when exceptions occur.[Learn more](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/essential/exceptions/tryResourceClose.html)Similar issue at line number 52.

Title: CWE-400,664 - Resource leak
Description: Allocated resources are not released properly. This can slow down or crash your system. They must be closed along all paths to prevent a resource leak.
Remediation: **Problem**This line of code contains a resource that might not be closed properly. This can cause a resource leak that slows down or crashes your system.**Fix**Consider closing the following resource: *con*. The resource is allocated by call *sqlite3.connect*. The resource is referenced at lines 20, 23. The resource is closed at line 23. There are other execution paths that do not contain closure statements, for example, when *Connection.cursor()* throws an exception. To prevent this resource leak, close *con* in a try-finally block or declare it using a `with` statement.**More info**[View details about the `with` statement in the Python developer's guide](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0343/) (external link).

Title: CWE-208 - Timing attack
Description: Operators like `==` and `===` are not time-safe and can make your application vulnerable to a timing attack, which might enable attackers to infer security-sensitive information.

Title: CWE-489 - Enabling and overriding debug feature

Title: CWE-329,1204,323 - Weak Initialization Vector (IV)
Description: Using a static or predictable initialization vector (IV) for a cryptographic cipher can make the resulting ciphertext much more predictable and susceptible to dictionary attacks.
Remediation: Using insecure random number generators can lead to predictable random values. This predictability can be exploited by attackers to guess sensitive values or break cryptographic operations. To fix this, use `new SecureRandom()` to instantiate a `java.security.SecureRandom` object instead of using non-cryptographic random number generators (like java.util.Random, Math.random(), or Apache Commons random utilities). For more information about secure random number generation, see: https://owasp.org/www-community/vulnerabilities/Insecure_Randomness

Secure your code by using framework functions like`getCanonicalPath()`, `normalize()`, or validating paths with`.startsWith()` checks. Learn more:https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/22.html

Title: CWE-400 - Denial of service rule
Description: A denial of service (DOS) attack can make it impossible for legitimate users to access computer systems, network, services or other resources.
Remediation: An attacker can provide an input stream or file that contains an excessive number of lines without a terminating condition. The call to `readLine()` might allow an attacker to crash the program or otherwise make it unavailable to legitimate users which causes denial of service. To avoid this, make sure you either enforce a limit on the lines read or apply proper input sanitization.

Title: CWE-22,23 - Path traversal
Description: Creating file paths from untrusted input could allow a malicious actor to access arbitrary files on a disk by manipulating the file name in the path.
Remediation: Path traversal vulnerability detected. An attacker could access files outside the intended directory, potentially exposing sensitive information or executing malicious code. Validate and sanitize file paths by using path.normalize() and checking that the resolved path is within the expected directory. Learn More - https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/22.html.

Title: CWE-798 - Hardcoded credentials
Description: Hardcoded credentials can be intercepted by malicious actors. Even after removing them from the code they may still pose a risk because an attacker might have recorded them to use them at a later point in time.
Remediation: It appears your code contains a hardcoded password. This poses a serious security risk as it may allow unauthorized access to sensitive data or systems. To remediate this issue, it is recommended to use AWS Secrets Manager to store and access secrets:	a) Visit Secrets Manager on the AWS Management Console.	b) Choose the secret type on the console page and follow the instructions.	c) Use the suggested code samples to retrieve the secret.To learn more about password management best practices, see [OWASP password storage cheat sheet](https://www.owasp.org/index.php/Password_Storage_Cheat_Sheet).

Title: CWE-703 - Improper error handling
Description: Improper error handling can enable attacks and lead to unwanted behavior.  Parts of the system may receive unintended input, which may result in altered control flow,  arbitrary control of a resource, or arbitrary code execution.
Remediation: Try, Except, Pass detected. https://bandit.readthedocs.io/en/latest/plugins/b110_try_except_pass.html

Title: CWE-79,80 - Cross-site scripting
Description: User-controllable input must be sanitized before it's included in output used to dynamically generate a web page. Unsanitized user input can introduce cross-side scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities that can lead to inadvertedly running malicious code in a trusted context.
Remediation: A potential Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability has been identified, where untrusted input is being reflected in the response without proper sanitization. This can allow an attacker to inject and execute malicious scripts in the user's browser, potentially compromising sensitive data or application behavior. To mitigate this risk, it is strongly recommended to sanitize or encode all user input before rendering it in the DOM or returning it in a response. Commonly used methods include DOMPurify.sanitize(), encodeURIComponent(), escape(), etc., depending on the use case and framework in use. Learn more - https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/79.html

Title: CWE-352,1275 - Cross-site request forgery
Description: Insecure configuration can lead to a cross-site request forgery (CSRF) vulnerability. This can enable an attacker to trick end users into performing unwanted actions while authenticated.
Remediation: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) protection is missing for state-changing operations. This vulnerability allows attackers to trick authenticated users into unknowingly performing unintended actions (e.g., changing passwords, transferring funds, or modifying data) by submitting malicious requests on their behalf. Implement anti-CSRF tokens for all POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE operations, or use SameSite cookies with 'strict' or 'lax' settings as an alternative defense. Reference: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Cross-Site_Request_Forgery_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html

Title: CWE-117,93 - Log injection
Description: User-provided inputs must be sanitized before they are logged. An attacker can use unsanitized input to break a log's integrity, forge log entries, or bypass log monitors.
Remediation: You have a log statement that might use unsanitized input originating from HTTP requests or AWS Lambda sources. Depending on the context, this could result in:1. A log injection attack that breaks log integrity, forges log entries, or bypasses monitors that use the logs. To increase the security of your code, sanitize your inputs before logging them. [Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/117.html)2. A sensitive information leak that exposes users' credentials, private information, or identifying information to an attacker. To preserve privacy in your code, redact sensitive user information before logging it. [Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/532.html)

Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule
Description: Resources that implement AutoCloseable or require explicit closure must be properly managed throughout their lifecycle. The recommended approach is to use try-with-resources block which ensures automatic and safe resource closure, regardless of whether an exception occurs or not. Failing to properly close resources can lead to resource leaks, memory issues, and system instability. This applies to both application resources and testing components.
Remediation: The code is not properly closing resources, which can lead to resource leaks. Unclosed resources may cause memory leaks or other system resource issues, potentially degrading application performance over time. If an exception occurs before the explicit close() call, the resource will not be closed, increasing the risk of leaks. To remediate this, use the try-with-resources statement to automatically close AutoCloseable resources.Alternatively, if try-with-resources cannot be used, ensure that close() is called in a finally block to guarantee proper resource cleanup even when exceptions occur.[Learn more](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/essential/exceptions/tryResourceClose.html)

Title: CWE-400,664 - Resource leak
Description: Allocated resources are not released properly. This can slow down or crash your system. They must be closed along all paths to prevent a resource leak.
Remediation: **Problem**This line of code contains a resource that might not be closed properly. This can cause a resource leak that slows down or crashes your system.**Fix**Consider closing the following resource: *conn*. The resource is allocated by call *sqlite3.connect*. The resource is referenced at line 13. The resource is returned at line 14. There are other execution paths that don't close the resource or return it, for example, when *Connection.execute()* throws an exception. To prevent this resource leak, close *conn* along these other paths before you exit this method.**More info**[View details about the `with` statement in the Python developer's guide](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0343/) (external link).

Title: CWE-329,1204,323 - Weak Initialization Vector (IV)
Description: Using a static or predictable initialization vector (IV) for a cryptographic cipher can make the resulting ciphertext much more predictable and susceptible to dictionary attacks.
Remediation: Using insecure random number generators can lead to predictable random values. This predictability can be exploited by attackers to guess sensitive values or break cryptographic operations. To fix this, use `new SecureRandom()` to instantiate a `java.security.SecureRandom` object instead of using non-cryptographic random number generators (like java.util.Random, Math.random(), or Apache Commons random utilities). For more information about secure random number generation, see: https://owasp.org/www-community/vulnerabilities/Insecure_Randomness

Title: CWE-327,328,326,208,1240 - Insecure cryptography
Description: Misuse of cryptography-related APIs can create security vulnerabilities. This includes one or more of the following: algorithms with known weaknesses, certain padding modes, lack of integrity checks, and insufficiently large key sizes.
Remediation: Your code uses a non-standard or weak password encoder, which could allow an attacker to gain access to protected data. To protect your code, use a standard and secure encoder like `BCryptPasswordEncoder`, `Pbkdf2PasswordEncoder`, `Argon2PasswordEncoder`, or `SCryptPasswordEncoder`. Avoid the use of the following encoders: `ShaPasswordEncoder`, `Md5PasswordEncoder`, `LdapShaPasswordEncoder`, `Md4PasswordEncoder`, `MessageDigestPasswordEncoder`, `NoOpPasswordEncoder`, and `StandardPasswordEncoder`.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/328.html)

Title: CWE-668 - Improper resource exposure
Description: Exposing resource information makes the resource vulnerable to attack.
Remediation: The rule flags the use of host `0.0.0.0` in application configuration, which exposes the server to all network interfaces, potentially including public networks. This configuration poses a security risk by making the application accessible to unauthorized users on the internet. Title: CWE-352,1275 - Cross-site request forgery
Description: Insecure configuration can lead to a cross-site request forgery (CSRF) vulnerability. This can enable an attacker to trick end users into performing unwanted actions while authenticated.
Remediation: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) protection is missing for state-changing operations. This vulnerability allows attackers to trick authenticated users into unknowingly performing unintended actions (e.g., changing passwords, transferring funds, or modifying data) by submitting malicious requests on their behalf. Implement anti-CSRF tokens for all POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE operations, or use SameSite cookies with 'strict' or 'lax' settings as an alternative defense. Reference: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Cross-Site_Request_Forgery_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html

Title: CWE-22,23 - Path traversal
Description: Creating file paths from untrusted input could allow a malicious actor to access arbitrary files on a disk by manipulating the file name in the path.

Validate and sanitize file paths by using path.normalize() and checking that the resolved path is within the expected directory. Learn More - https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/22.html.

Title: CWE-20,79,80 - Cross-site scripting

Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule
Description: Resources that implement AutoCloseable or require explicit closure must be properly managed throughout their lifecycle. The recommended approach is to use try-with-resources block which ensures automatic and safe resource closure, regardless of whether an exception occurs or not. Failing to properly close resources can lead to resource leaks, memory issues, and system instability. This applies to both application resources and testing components.
Remediation: The code is not properly closing resources, which can lead to resource leaks. Unclosed resources may cause memory leaks or other system resource issues, potentially degrading application performance over time. If an exception occurs before the explicit close() call, the resource will not be closed, increasing the risk of leaks. To remediate this, use the try-with-resources statement to automatically close AutoCloseable resources.Alternatively, if try-with-resources cannot be used, ensure that close() is called in a finally block to guarantee proper resource cleanup even when exceptions occur.[Learn more](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/essential/exceptions/tryResourceClose.html)

Title: CWE-329,1204,323 - Weak Initialization Vector (IV)
Description: Using a static or predictable initialization vector (IV) for a cryptographic cipher can make the resulting ciphertext much more predictable and susceptible to dictionary attacks.
Remediation: Using insecure random number generators can lead to predictable random values. To fix this, use `new SecureRandom()` to instantiate a `java.security.SecureRandom` object instead of using non-cryptographic random number generators (like java.util.Random, Math.random(), or Apache Commons random utilities). For more information about secure random number generation, see: https://owasp.org/www-community/vulnerabilities/Insecure_Randomness

Title: CWE-770 - Unbounded Thread Pool Configuration
Description: ExecutorService configurations with unbounded thread pools or queues can result in uncontrolled resource allocation. This can lead to system instability, out-of-memory errors, and potential denial of service under high load conditions. Unbounded queues may accumulate tasks indefinitely, consuming excessive memory. Validate and sanitize file paths by using path.normalize() and checking that the resolved path is within the expected directory. Learn More - https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/22.html.

Title: CWE-276 - Incorrect default Permissions
Description: Incorrect default permissions in JavaScript code can lead to serious security issues, including unauthorized access, data exposure, or modification of sensitive files. Proper setting of file and directory permissions, along with restrictive defaults (e.g., 0o600 for files or 0o700 for directories), is essential to ensure that only authorized users have access. Explicit permission management mitigates risks associated with relying on system defaults, which may be too permissive.
Remediation: Insecure file permissions (0o777, 0o666, 0o755, 0o644) detected. These can allow unauthorized users to read, write, or execute the file, leading to data breaches or privilege escalation. Use more restrictive permissions such as 0o600 (read-write for owner only) to ensure proper security. For more details on secure file permissions, refer to [CWE-276](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/276.html).

Title: CWE-306 - Missing Authentication for Critical Function

The lack of authentication checks can cause the exposure of information, denial of service, and arbitrary code execution. We recommend that you apply authentication checks to all access points.
Remediation: This route is missing an authentication check, which may allow unauthorized access to sensitive functionality. Ensure that authentication middleware or mechanisms are applied to protect critical routes. Failing to do so can lead to security vulnerabilities such as unauthorized access. Learn more: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Authentication_Cheat_Sheet.html

Title: Do not fetch sensitive information from a GET request.

Title: CWE-400 - Denial of service rule
Description: A denial of service (DOS) attack can make it impossible for legitimate users to access computer systems, network, services or other resources.
Remediation: An attacker can provide an input stream or file that contains an excessive number of lines without a terminating condition. Title: CWE-329,1204,323 - Weak Initialization Vector (IV)
Description: Using a static or predictable initialization vector (IV) for a cryptographic cipher can make the resulting ciphertext much more predictable and susceptible to dictionary attacks.
Remediation: Using insecure random number generators can lead to predictable random values. This predictability can be exploited by attackers to guess sensitive values or break cryptographic operations. To fix this, use `new SecureRandom()` to instantiate a `java.security.SecureRandom` object instead of using non-cryptographic random number generators (like java.util.Random, Math.random(), or Apache Commons random utilities). For more information about secure random number generation, see: https://owasp.org/www-community/vulnerabilities/Insecure_Randomness

Title: CWE-918 - Server-side request forgery
Description: Insufficient sanitization of potentially untrusted URLs on the server side can lead to the server issuing requests to unwanted hosts, ports, or protocols, which can bypass proxies, firewalls, and other security measures.
Remediation: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) vulnerability detected.  Untrusted user input is being used in a network request (in fetch, axios, or http.request)  without proper validation. This can allow attackers to craft requests to internal systems, access metadata services,  or perform internal port scans. To fix this issue, strictly validate and sanitize the input URL  using a library like `node:url` for parsing, enforce an allowlist of trusted domains,  and block access to private IP ranges (e.g., 127.0.0.1, 169.254.169.254).  Learn more: https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/Server_Side_Request_Forgery

Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule

Remediation: Disabling cross-site request forgery (CSRF) protection can make your users vulnerable to attacks. Enable CSRF if your application can be accessed through a browser and another token mechanism isn't in place.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/352.html)

Title: CWE-489 - Enabling and overriding debug feature
Description: Don't enable or override an application's debug feature. Instead, use OS environment variables to set up the debug feature.
Remediation: Don't hardcode the `DEBUG` or `SECRET_KEY` variable because some system extensions might be configured to use a different value. Instead, use OS environment variables to set up the debug feature.

Title: CWE-352,1275 - Cross-site request forgery
Description: Insecure configuration can lead to a cross-site request forgery (CSRF) vulnerability. This can enable an attacker to trick end users into performing unwanted actions while authenticated.
Remediation: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) protection is missing for state-changing operations. This vulnerability allows attackers to trick authenticated users into unknowingly performing unintended actions (e.g., changing passwords, transferring funds, or modifying data) by submitting malicious requests on their behalf. Implement anti-CSRF tokens for all POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE operations, or use SameSite cookies with 'strict' or 'lax' settings as an alternative defense. Reference: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Cross-Site_Request_Forgery_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html

Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule
Description: Resources that implement AutoCloseable or require explicit closure must be properly managed throughout their lifecycle. The recommended approach is to use try-with-resources block which ensures automatic and safe resource closure, regardless of whether an exception occurs or not. Failing to properly close resources can lead to resource leaks, memory issues, and system instability. This applies to both application resources and testing components.
Remediation: The code is not properly closing resources, which can lead to resource leaks. Unclosed resources may cause memory leaks or other system resource issues, potentially degrading application performance over time. If an exception occurs before the explicit close() call, the resource will not be closed, increasing the risk of leaks. To remediate this, use the try-with-resources statement to automatically close AutoCloseable resources.Alternatively, if try-with-resources cannot be used, ensure that close() is called in a finally block to guarantee proper resource cleanup even when exceptions occur.[Learn more](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/essential/exceptions/tryResourceClose.html)

Title: CWE-22 - Path traversal
Description: Constructing path names with unsanitized user input can lead to path traversal attacks (for example, `../../..`) that allow an attacker access to file system resources.
Remediation: Path traversal vulnerability detected. User-controlled input in file paths can allow attackers to access files outside intended directories using `../` sequences. Secure your code by using framework functions like `safe_join()`, `secure_filename()` and `.startswith()` checks. Learn more: https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/22.html

Title: CWE-22,23 - Path traversal
Description: Creating file paths from untrusted input might give a malicious actor access to sensitive files by manipulating the file name to access an arbitrary file on disk.
Remediation: Path traversal vulnerability detected. User-controlled input in file pathscan allow attackers to access files outside intended directories using`../` sequences. Secure your code by using framework functions like`getCanonicalPath()`, `normalize()`, or validating paths with`.startsWith()` checks. Learn more:https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/22.html

Title: CWE-117,93 - Log injection
Description: User-provided inputs must be sanitized before they are logged. An attacker can use unsanitized input to break a log's integrity, forge log entries, or bypass log monitors.
Remediation: Potential log injection detected. Ensure all untrusted input is properly sanitized before logging. Use parameterized logging or validate input against a allow list to prevent log injection vulnerabilities. Consider using a dedicated logging library's built-in sanitization features when available. Learn more - https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/117.html

Title: CWE-329,1204,323 - Weak Initialization Vector (IV)
Description: Using a static or predictable initialization vector (IV) for a cryptographic cipher can make the resulting ciphertext much more predictable and susceptible to dictionary attacks.
Remediation: Using insecure random number generators can lead to predictable random values. This predictability can be exploited by attackers to guess sensitive values or break cryptographic operations. To fix this, use `new SecureRandom()` to instantiate a `java.security.SecureRandom` object instead of using non-cryptographic random number generators (like java.util.Random, Math.random(), or Apache Commons random utilities). For more information about secure random number generation, see: https://owasp.org/www-community/vulnerabilities/Insecure_Randomness

Title: CWE-601 - URL redirection to untrusted site
Description: An HTTP parameter could contain a URL value and cause the web application to redirect the request to the specified URL. By modifying the URL value to a malicious site, an attacker could successfully launch a phishing attack and steal user credentials.
Remediation: It looks like you are performing http redirect from an untrusted user input. This might enable attackers to redirect users to a malicious site. User provided data such as URL parameters, POST data payloads, or cookies should always be considered untrusted and must be validated to ensure safe redirection.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/601.html)

Title: Do not fetch sensitive information from a GET request.
Description: Do not fetch sensitive information from a GET request.
Remediation: It looks like you are using query parameters to fetch sensitive information. Placing sensitive data in a GET request increases the risk of the data being exposed to an attacker. It is advised to read such data from a POST request body.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/598.html)

Title: CWE-400 - Denial of service rule
Description: A denial of service (DOS) attack can make it impossible for legitimate users to access computer systems, network, services or other resources.
Remediation: An attacker can provide an input stream or file that contains an excessive number of lines without a terminating condition. The call to `readLine()` might allow an attacker to crash the program or otherwise make it unavailable to legitimate users which causes denial of service. To avoid this, make sure you either enforce a limit on the lines read or apply proper input sanitization.

Title: CWE-400,664 - Resource leak
Description: Allocated resources are not released properly. This can slow down or crash your system. They must be closed along all paths to prevent a resource leak.
Remediation: **Problem**This line of code contains a resource that might not be closed properly. This can cause a resource leak that slows down or crashes your system.**Fix**Consider closing the following resource: *con*. The resource is allocated by call *sqlite3.connect*. The resource is referenced at lines 35, 36, 37. The resource is closed at line 37. There are other execution paths that do not contain closure statements, for example, when *Connection.commit()* throws an exception. To prevent this resource leak, close *con* in a try-finally block or declare it using a `with` statement.**More info**[View details about the `with` statement in the Python developer's guide](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0343/) (external link).

Title: CWE-918 - Server-side request forgery
Description: Insufficient sanitization of potentially untrusted URLs on the server side can lead to the server issuing requests to unwanted hosts, ports, or protocols, which can bypass proxies, firewalls, and other security measures.
Remediation: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) vulnerability detected.  Untrusted user input is being used in a network request (in fetch, axios, or http.request)  without proper validation. This can allow attackers to craft requests to internal systems, access metadata services,  or perform internal port scans. To fix this issue, strictly validate and sanitize the input URL  using a library like `node:url` for parsing, enforce an allowlist of trusted domains,  and block access to private IP ranges (e.g., 127.0.0.1, 169.254.169.254).  Learn more: https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/Server_Side_Request_Forgery

Title: CWE-94 - Unsanitized input is run as code
Description: Running scripts generated from unsanitized inputs (for example, evaluating expressions that include user-provided strings) can lead to malicious behavior and inadvertently running code remotely.
Remediation: This code is vulnerable to code injection because it executes user-controlled input without proper validation or sanitization. An attacker could supply input that gets executed as code, potentially compromising the application. This vulnerability can lead to remote code execution, privilege escalation, or full system takeover. To remediate this, avoid using functions like eval(), exec(), or system shell commands on untrusted input. In Python, prefer ast.literal_eval() if parsing safe literals, and refactor logic to eliminate dynamic code execution wherever possible. Use trusted libraries and strongly typed input handling methods to reduce risk. More information - https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/Code_Injection
Detector Name: Unsanitized input is run as code

To make your code secure, do not enable the debug or test feature on production servers. For more information, see [CWE-489](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/489.html).

Title: CWE-329,1204,323 - Weak Initialization Vector (IV)
Description: Using a static or predictable initialization vector (IV) for a cryptographic cipher can make the resulting ciphertext much more predictable and susceptible to dictionary attacks.
Remediation: Using insecure random number generators can lead to predictable random values. This predictability can be exploited by attackers to guess sensitive values or break cryptographic operations. To fix this, use `new SecureRandom()` to instantiate a `java.security.SecureRandom` object instead of using non-cryptographic random number generators (like java.util.Random, Math.random(), or Apache Commons random utilities). For more information about secure random number generation, see: https://owasp.org/www-community/vulnerabilities/Insecure_Randomness

Title: CWE-798 - Hardcoded credentials
Description: Hardcoded credentials can be intercepted by malicious actors. Even after removing them from the code they may still pose a risk because an attacker might have recorded them to use them at a later point in time.
Remediation: It appears your code may contain a hardcoded secret. We recommend replacing it with AWS Secrets Manager references to enhance security and follow best practices. For more information, please refer [OWASP password storage cheat sheet](https://www.owasp.org/index.php/Password_Storage_Cheat_Sheet).

Title: CWE-117,93 - Log injection
Description: User-provided inputs must be sanitized before they are logged. An attacker can use unsanitized input to break a log's integrity, forge log entries, or bypass log monitors.
Remediation: Potential log injection detected. Ensure all untrusted input is properly sanitized before logging. Use parameterized logging or validate input against a allow list to prevent log injection vulnerabilities. Consider using a dedicated logging library's built-in sanitization features when available. Learn more - https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/117.html

Title: CWE-798 - Hardcoded credentials
Description: Hardcoded credentials can be intercepted by malicious actors. Even after removing them from the code they may still pose a risk because an attacker might have recorded them to use them at a later point in time.

Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule
Description: Resources that implement AutoCloseable or require explicit closure must be properly managed throughout their lifecycle. The recommended approach is to use try-with-resources block which ensures automatic and safe resource closure, regardless of whether an exception occurs or not. Failing to properly close resources can lead to resource leaks, memory issues, and system instability. This applies to both application resources and testing components.
Remediation: The code is not properly closing resources, which can lead to resource leaks. Unclosed resources may cause memory leaks or other system resource issues, potentially degrading application performance over time. If an exception occurs before the explicit close() call, the resource will not be closed, increasing the risk of leaks. To remediate this, use the try-with-resources statement to automatically close AutoCloseable resources.Alternatively, if try-with-resources cannot be used, ensure that close() is called in a finally block to guarantee proper resource cleanup even when exceptions occur.[Learn more](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/essential/exceptions/tryResourceClose.html)

Title: CWE-94 - Unsanitized input is run as code
Description: Running scripts generated from unsanitized inputs (for example, evaluating expressions that include user-provided strings) can lead to malicious behavior and inadvertently running code remotely.

Title: CWE-352,1275 - Cross-site request forgery
Description: Insecure configuration can lead to a cross-site request forgery (CSRF) vulnerability. This can enable an attacker to trick end users into performing unwanted actions while authenticated.
Remediation: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) protection is missing for state-changing operations. This vulnerability allows attackers to trick authenticated users into unknowingly performing unintended actions (e.g., changing passwords, transferring funds, or modifying data) by submitting malicious requests on their behalf. Implement anti-CSRF tokens for all POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE operations, or use SameSite cookies with 'strict' or 'lax' settings as an alternative defense. Reference: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Cross-Site_Request_Forgery_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html

While using the sensitive trait will result in redacting the data marked as sensitive from the Coral request logs, it will have no effect if the data is logged in the application log.
Remediation: This code logs sensitive information (such as PAN numbers, credit cards, or access tokens) which could expose confidential data to unauthorized personnel through system logs and monitoring tools. Such exposure could lead to financial fraud, identity theft, or regulatory violations. Consider using data masking or removing sensitive information from log statements to maintain data security.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/200.html)

Title: CWE-352,1275 - Cross-site request forgery
Description: Insecure configuration can lead to a cross-site request forgery (CSRF) vulnerability. This can enable an attacker to trick end users into performing unwanted actions while authenticated.
Remediation: Disabling cross-site request forgery (CSRF) protection can make your users vulnerable to attacks. Enable CSRF if your application can be accessed through a browser and another token mechanism isn't in place.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/352.html)

Title: CWE-400,664 - Resource leak
Description: Allocated resources are not released properly. This can slow down or crash your system. They must be closed along all paths to prevent a resource leak.
Remediation: **Problem**This line of code contains a resource that might not be closed properly. This can cause a resource leak that slows down or crashes your system.**Fix**Consider closing the following resource: *conn*. The resource is referenced at line 40. The resource is closed at line 51. Title: CWE-117,93 - Log injection
Description: User-provided inputs must be sanitized before they are logged. An attacker can use unsanitized input to break a log's integrity, forge log entries, or bypass log monitors.
Remediation: Potential log injection detected. Ensure all untrusted input is properly sanitized before logging. Use parameterized logging or validate input against a allow list to prevent log injection vulnerabilities. Consider using a dedicated logging library's built-in sanitization features when available. Learn more - https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/117.html

Title: CWE-798 - Hardcoded credentials
Description: Hardcoded credentials can be intercepted by malicious actors. Even after removing them from the code they may still pose a risk because an attacker might have recorded them to use them at a later point in time.
Remediation: It appears your code may contain a hardcoded secret. We recommend replacing it with AWS Secrets Manager references to enhance security and follow best practices. For more information, please refer [OWASP password storage cheat sheet](https://www.owasp.org/index.php/Password_Storage_Cheat_Sheet).

Title: CWE-94 - Unsanitized input is run as code
Description: Running scripts generated from unsanitized inputs (for example, evaluating expressions that include user-provided strings) can lead to malicious behavior and inadvertently running code remotely.
Remediation: This code is vulnerable to code injection because it executes user-controlled input without proper validation or sanitization. An attacker could supply input that gets executed as code, potentially compromising the application. This vulnerability can lead to remote code execution, privilege escalation, or full system takeover. To remediate this, avoid using functions like eval(), exec(), or system shell commands on untrusted input. In Python, prefer ast.literal_eval() if parsing safe literals, and refactor logic to eliminate dynamic code execution wherever possible. Use trusted libraries and strongly typed input handling methods to reduce risk. More information - https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/Code_Injection
Detector Name: Unsanitized input is run as code

Title: CWE-614 - Insecure cookie

Title: CWE-20,79,80 - Cross-site scripting

Title: CWE-276 - Incorrect default Permissions
Description: Incorrect default permissions in JavaScript code can lead to serious security issues, including unauthorized access, data exposure, or modification of sensitive files. Proper setting of file and directory permissions, along with restrictive defaults (e.g., 0o600 for files or 0o700 for directories), is essential to ensure that only authorized users have access. Explicit permission management mitigates risks associated with relying on system defaults, which may be too permissive.
Remediation: Insecure file permissions (0o777, 0o666, 0o755, 0o644) detected. These can allow unauthorized users to read, write, or execute the file, leading to data breaches or privilege escalation. Use more restrictive permissions such as 0o600 (read-write for owner only) to ensure proper security. For more details on secure file permissions, refer to [CWE-276](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/276.html).

Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule
Description: Resources that implement AutoCloseable or require explicit closure must be properly managed throughout their lifecycle. The recommended approach is to use try-with-resources block which ensures automatic and safe resource closure, regardless of whether an exception occurs or not. Failing to properly close resources can lead to resource leaks, memory issues, and system instability. This applies to both application resources and testing components.
Remediation: The code is not properly closing resources, which can lead to resource leaks. Unclosed resources may cause memory leaks or other system resource issues, potentially degrading application performance over time. If an exception occurs before the explicit close() call, the resource will not be closed, increasing the risk of leaks. To remediate this, use the try-with-resources statement to automatically close AutoCloseable resources.Alternatively, if try-with-resources cannot be used, ensure that close() is called in a finally block to guarantee proper resource cleanup even when exceptions occur.[Learn more](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/essential/exceptions/tryResourceClose.html)

Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule
Description: Resources that implement AutoCloseable or require explicit closure must be properly managed throughout their lifecycle. The recommended approach is to use try-with-resources block which ensures automatic and safe resource closure, regardless of whether an exception occurs or not. Failing to properly close resources can lead to resource leaks, memory issues, and system instability. This applies to both application resources and testing components.
Remediation: The code is not properly closing resources, which can lead to resource leaks. Unclosed resources may cause memory leaks or other system resource issues, potentially degrading application performance over time. If an exception occurs before the explicit close() call, the resource will not be closed, increasing the risk of leaks. To remediate this, use the try-with-resources statement to automatically close AutoCloseable resources.Alternatively, if try-with-resources cannot be used, ensure that close() is called in a finally block to guarantee proper resource cleanup even when exceptions occur.[Learn more](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/essential/exceptions/tryResourceClose.html)Similar issue at line number 10.

Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule
Description: Resources that implement AutoCloseable or require explicit closure must be properly managed throughout their lifecycle. The recommended approach is to use try-with-resources block which ensures automatic and safe resource closure, regardless of whether an exception occurs or not. Failing to properly close resources can lead to resource leaks, memory issues, and system instability. This applies to both application resources and testing components.
Remediation: The code is not properly closing resources, which can lead to resource leaks. Unclosed resources may cause memory leaks or other system resource issues, potentially degrading application performance over time. If an exception occurs before the explicit close() call, the resource will not be closed, increasing the risk of leaks. To remediate this, use the try-with-resources statement to automatically close AutoCloseable resources.Alternatively, if try-with-resources cannot be used, ensure that close() is called in a finally block to guarantee proper resource cleanup even when exceptions occur.[Learn more](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/essential/exceptions/tryResourceClose.html)Similar issue at line number 27.

Description: Type confusion occurs when an application accesses a resource using a type that is incompatible with its the original type. Such a vulnerability can allow attackers to tamper request parameters and bypass sanitizers used in the application code.
Remediation: It appears that you are using substring methods like `includes`, `indexOf` and `lastIndexOf` on an external request parameter, assuming that it is of type `String`. This can enable malicious actors to bypass the check by modifying the request parameter to contain an `Array` instead of a `String` value. We recommend that you use the `typeof` operator to make sure that the request parameter is of type `String` before applying substring methods on it.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/843.html)

Title: CWE-352,1275 - Cross-site request forgery
Description: Insecure configuration can lead to a cross-site request forgery (CSRF) vulnerability. This can enable an attacker to trick end users into performing unwanted actions while authenticated.
Remediation: Disabling cross-site request forgery (CSRF) protection can make your users vulnerable to attacks. Enable CSRF if your application can be accessed through a browser and another token mechanism isn't in place.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/352.html)

Title: CWE-329,1204,323 - Weak Initialization Vector (IV)
Description: Using a static or predictable initialization vector (IV) for a cryptographic cipher can make the resulting ciphertext much more predictable and susceptible to dictionary attacks.
Remediation: Using insecure random number generators can lead to predictable random values. This predictability can be exploited by attackers to guess sensitive values or break cryptographic operations. To fix this, use `new SecureRandom()` to instantiate a `java.security.SecureRandom` object instead of using non-cryptographic random number generators (like java.util.Random, Math.random(), or Apache Commons random utilities). For more information about secure random number generation, see: https://owasp.org/www-community/vulnerabilities/Insecure_Randomness

Title: CWE-79,80 - Cross-site scripting
Description: User-controllable input must be sanitized before it's included in output used to dynamically generate a web page. Unsanitized user input can introduce cross-side scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities that can lead to inadvertedly running malicious code in a trusted context.
Remediation: A potential Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability has been identified, where untrusted input is being reflected in the response without proper sanitization. This can allow an attacker to inject and execute malicious scripts in the user's browser, potentially compromising sensitive data or application behavior. To mitigate this risk, it is strongly recommended to sanitize or encode all user input before rendering it in the DOM or returning it in a response. Commonly used methods include DOMPurify.sanitize(), encodeURIComponent(), escape(), etc., depending on the use case and framework in use. Learn more - https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/79.html
