Title: CWE-918 - Server-side request forgery
Description: Insufficient sanitization of potentially untrusted URLs on the server side can lead to the server issuing requests to unwanted hosts, ports, or protocols, which can bypass proxies, firewalls, and other security measures.
Remediation: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) vulnerability detected.  Untrusted user input is being used in a network request (in fetch, axios, or http.request)  without proper validation. This can allow attackers to craft requests to internal systems, access metadata services,  or perform internal port scans. To fix this issue, strictly validate and sanitize the input URL  using a library like `node:url` for parsing, enforce an allowlist of trusted domains,  and block access to private IP ranges (e.g., 127.0.0.1, 169.254.169.254).  Learn more: https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/Server_Side_Request_Forgery

Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule
Description: Resources that implement AutoCloseable or require explicit closure must be properly managed throughout their lifecycle. The recommended approach is to use try-with-resources block which ensures automatic and safe resource closure, regardless of whether an exception occurs or not. Failing to properly close resources can lead to resource leaks, memory issues, and system instability. This applies to both application resources and testing components.
Remediation: The code is not properly closing resources, which can lead to resource leaks. Unclosed resources may cause memory leaks or other system resource issues, potentially degrading application performance over time. If an exception occurs before the explicit close() call, the resource will not be closed, increasing the risk of leaks. To remediate this, use the try-with-resources statement to automatically close AutoCloseable resources.Alternatively, if try-with-resources cannot be used, ensure that close() is called in a finally block to guarantee proper resource cleanup even when exceptions occur.[Learn more](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/essential/exceptions/tryResourceClose.html)

Title: CWE-22,23 - Path traversal
Description: Creating file paths from untrusted input might give a malicious actor access to sensitive files by manipulating the file name to access an arbitrary file on disk.
Remediation: Path traversal vulnerability detected. User-controlled input in file pathscan allow attackers to access files outside intended directories using`../` sequences. Secure your code by using framework functions like`getCanonicalPath()`, `normalize()`, or validating paths with`.startsWith()` checks. Learn more:https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/22.html

Title: CWE-770 - Unbounded Thread Pool Configuration
Description: ExecutorService configurations with unbounded thread pools or queues can result in uncontrolled resource allocation. This can lead to system instability, out-of-memory errors, and potential denial of service under high load conditions. Unbounded queues may accumulate tasks indefinitely, consuming excessive memory. Thread pools with excessively high or unlimited maximum thread counts can create an unsustainable number of threads, leading to degraded performance due to excessive context switching and resource contention.
Remediation: Non-daemon thread creation detected. Non-daemon threads prevent JVM from exiting until they complete, which can cause service hang during shutdown. Configure threads as daemon threads using setDaemon(true) to ensure clean service termination. Learn more: https://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/api/java/lang/Thread.html#setDaemon-boolean-

Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule
Description: Resources that implement AutoCloseable or require explicit closure must be properly managed throughout their lifecycle. The recommended approach is to use try-with-resources block which ensures automatic and safe resource closure, regardless of whether an exception occurs or not. Failing to properly close resources can lead to resource leaks, memory issues, and system instability. This applies to both application resources and testing components.
Remediation: The code is not properly closing resources, which can lead to resource leaks. Unclosed resources may cause memory leaks or other system resource issues, potentially degrading application performance over time. If an exception occurs before the explicit close() call, the resource will not be closed, increasing the risk of leaks. To remediate this, use the try-with-resources statement to automatically close AutoCloseable resources.Alternatively, if try-with-resources cannot be used, ensure that close() is called in a finally block to guarantee proper resource cleanup even when exceptions occur.[Learn more](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/essential/exceptions/tryResourceClose.html)

Title: CWE-352,1275 - Cross-site request forgery
Description: Insecure configuration can lead to a cross-site request forgery (CSRF) vulnerability. This can enable an attacker to trick end users into performing unwanted actions while authenticated.
Remediation: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) protection is missing for state-changing operations. This vulnerability allows attackers to trick authenticated users into unknowingly performing unintended actions (e.g., changing passwords, transferring funds, or modifying data) by submitting malicious requests on their behalf. Implement anti-CSRF tokens for all POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE operations, or use SameSite cookies with 'strict' or 'lax' settings as an alternative defense. Reference: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Cross-Site_Request_Forgery_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html

Title: CWE-79 - Reflected cross site scripting
Description: Rule for detecting reflected cross-site scripting in code that uses the Spring or Horizonte framework.
Remediation: A cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability was detected in your code, allowing an attacker to run arbitrary JavaScript in users' browser. The attacker could steal session cookies, download and install malicious files, redirect users to malicious domains, and more.Always assume all inputs are malicious, such as user-provided parameters, hidden fields, cookies, headers, and the URL. Use allow-list filtering, perform proper output encoding, escaping, and quoting against all inputs.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/79.html)

Title: CWE-400,664 - Resource leak

Remediation: Untrusted data must be properly encoded or sanitized before beingincorporated into web page content or used to generate dynamic output.Failure to do so can result in Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities,enabling attackers to inject malicious scripts into the application. Thesescripts, when executed in users' browsers, can lead to various securitybreaches such as session hijacking, data theft, or unauthorized actionsperformed within the victim's session context. To mitigate this risk,use built-in template escaping mechanisms, HTML escape functions like`html.escape()`, or dedicated security libraries such as Bleach.Learn more: https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/xss/

Title: CWE-117,93 - Log injection
Description: User-provided inputs must be sanitized before they are logged. An attacker can use unsanitized input to break a log's integrity, forge log entries, or bypass log monitors.
Remediation: Potential log injection detected. Ensure all untrusted input is properly sanitized before logging. Use parameterized logging or validate input against a allow list to prevent log injection vulnerabilities. Consider using a dedicated logging library's built-in sanitization features when available. Learn more - https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/117.html

Title: CWE-352,1275 - Cross-site request forgery
Description: Insecure configuration can lead to a cross-site request forgery (CSRF) vulnerability. This can enable an attacker to trick end users into performing unwanted actions while authenticated.
Remediation: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) protection is missing for state-changing operations. This vulnerability allows attackers to trick authenticated users into unknowingly performing unintended actions (e.g., changing passwords, transferring funds, or modifying data) by submitting malicious requests on their behalf. Implement anti-CSRF tokens for all POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE operations, or use SameSite cookies with 'strict' or 'lax' settings as an alternative defense. Reference: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Cross-Site_Request_Forgery_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html

Title: CWE-798 - Hardcoded credentials
Description: Hardcoded credentials can be intercepted by malicious actors. Even after removing them from the code they may still pose a risk because an attacker might have recorded them to use them at a later point in time.
Remediation: It appears your code contains a hardcoded password. This poses a serious security risk as it may allow unauthorized access to sensitive data or systems. To remediate this issue, it is recommended to use AWS Secrets Manager to store and access secrets:	a) Visit Secrets Manager on the AWS Management Console.	b) Choose the secret type on the console page and follow the instructions.	c) Use the suggested code samples to retrieve the secret.To learn more about password management best practices, see [OWASP password storage cheat sheet](https://www.owasp.org/index.php/Password_Storage_Cheat_Sheet).

Title: CWE-276 - Incorrect default Permissions
Description: Incorrect default permissions in python code can lead to serious security issues, including unauthorized access, data exposure, or modification of sensitive files. Proper setting of file and directory permissions, along with restrictive defaults (e.g., 0o600 for files or 0o700 for directories), is essential to ensure that only authorized users have access. Explicit permission management mitigates risks associated with relying on system defaults, which may be too permissive.

Title: CWE-489 - Enabling and overriding debug feature
Description: Don't enable or override an application's debug feature. Instead, use OS environment variables to set up the debug feature.
Remediation: Enabling an application's debug or test feature makes it easier for developers to find bugs, but it also gives attackers access to detailed information about both the system running the application and users. To make your code secure, do not enable the debug or test feature on production servers. For more information, see [CWE-489](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/489.html).

Title: CWE-200 - Application logging of sensitive data
Description: ACAT detected logging of data marked as sensitive using the sensitive trait in the corresponding Coral model. While using the sensitive trait will result in redacting the data marked as sensitive from the Coral request logs, it will have no effect if the data is logged in the application log.
Remediation: This code logs sensitive information (such as PAN numbers, credit cards, or access tokens) which could expose confidential data to unauthorized personnel through system logs and monitoring tools. Such exposure could lead to financial fraud, identity theft, or regulatory violations. Consider using data masking or removing sensitive information from log statements to maintain data security.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/200.html)

Title: CWE-329,1204,323 - Weak Initialization Vector (IV)
Description: Using a static or predictable initialization vector (IV) for a cryptographic cipher can make the resulting ciphertext much more predictable and susceptible to dictionary attacks.
Remediation: Using insecure random number generators can lead to predictable random values. This predictability can be exploited by attackers to guess sensitive values or break cryptographic operations. To fix this, use `new SecureRandom()` to instantiate a `java.security.SecureRandom` object instead of using non-cryptographic random number generators (like java.util.Random, Math.random(), or Apache Commons random utilities). For more information about secure random number generation, see: https://owasp.org/www-community/vulnerabilities/Insecure_Randomness

Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule
Description: Resources that implement AutoCloseable or require explicit closure must be properly managed throughout their lifecycle. The recommended approach is to use try-with-resources block which ensures automatic and safe resource closure, regardless of whether an exception occurs or not. Failing to properly close resources can lead to resource leaks, memory issues, and system instability. This applies to both application resources and testing components.
Remediation: The code is not properly closing resources, which can lead to resource leaks. Unclosed resources may cause memory leaks or other system resource issues, potentially degrading application performance over time. If an exception occurs before the explicit close() call, the resource will not be closed, increasing the risk of leaks. To remediate this, use the try-with-resources statement to automatically close AutoCloseable resources.Alternatively, if try-with-resources cannot be used, ensure that close() is called in a finally block to guarantee proper resource cleanup even when exceptions occur.[Learn more](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/essential/exceptions/tryResourceClose.html)

Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule
Description: Resources that implement AutoCloseable or require explicit closure must be properly managed throughout their lifecycle. The recommended approach is to use try-with-resources block which ensures automatic and safe resource closure, regardless of whether an exception occurs or not. Failing to properly close resources can lead to resource leaks, memory issues, and system instability. This applies to both application resources and testing components.
Remediation: The code is not properly closing resources, which can lead to resource leaks. Unclosed resources may cause memory leaks or other system resource issues, potentially degrading application performance over time. If an exception occurs before the explicit close() call, the resource will not be closed, increasing the risk of leaks. To remediate this, use the try-with-resources statement to automatically close AutoCloseable resources.Alternatively, if try-with-resources cannot be used, ensure that close() is called in a finally block to guarantee proper resource cleanup even when exceptions occur.[Learn more](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/essential/exceptions/tryResourceClose.html)

This vulnerability allows attackers to trick authenticated users into unknowingly performing unintended actions (e.g., changing passwords, transferring funds, or modifying data) by submitting malicious requests on their behalf. Implement anti-CSRF tokens for all POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE operations, or use SameSite cookies with 'strict' or 'lax' settings as an alternative defense. Reference: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Cross-Site_Request_Forgery_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html

Title: CWE-352,1275 - Cross-site request forgery
Description: Insecure configuration can lead to a cross-site request forgery (CSRF) vulnerability. This can enable an attacker to trick end users into performing unwanted actions while authenticated.
Remediation: We detected that your application is configured to send cookies across both first-party and cross-site requests, which can lead to cross-site request forgery (CSRF) attacks. We recommend that you set the `sameSite` attribute to `lax`, `strict`, or `true`.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/1275.html)

Title: CWE-79,80 - Cross-site scripting
Description: User-controllable input must be sanitized before it's included in output used to dynamically generate a web page. Unsanitized user input can introduce cross-side scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities that can lead to inadvertedly running malicious code in a trusted context.
Remediation: A potential Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability has been identified, where untrusted input is being reflected in the response without proper sanitization. This can allow an attacker to inject and execute malicious scripts in the user's browser, potentially compromising sensitive data or application behavior. To mitigate this risk, it is strongly recommended to sanitize or encode all user input before rendering it in the DOM or returning it in a response. Commonly used methods include DOMPurify.sanitize(), encodeURIComponent(), escape(), etc., depending on the use case and framework in use. Learn more - https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/79.html

Title: CWE-22 - Path traversal
Description: Constructing path names with unsanitized user input can lead to path traversal attacks (for example, `../../..`) that allow an attacker access to file system resources.
Remediation: Path traversal vulnerability detected. User-controlled input in file paths can allow attackers to access files outside intended directories using `../` sequences. Secure your code by using framework functions like `safe_join()`, `secure_filename()` and `.startswith()` checks. Learn more: https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/22.html

Title: CWE-798 - Hardcoded credentials
Description: Hardcoded credentials can be intercepted by malicious actors. Even after removing them from the code they may still pose a risk because an attacker might have recorded them to use them at a later point in time.
Remediation: We detected a hardcoded database connection password in your code. This could allow anyone with access to your server to perform privileged actions on the database. We recommend that you avoid hardcoded passwords when connecting to a database and instead use environment variables or a configuration file to set the password.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/259.html)

Title: CWE-117,93 - Log injection
Description: User-provided inputs must be sanitized before they are logged. An attacker can use unsanitized input to break a log's integrity, forge log entries, or bypass log monitors.
Remediation: Potential log injection detected. Ensure all untrusted input is properly sanitized before logging. Title: CWE-400 - Denial of service rule
Description: A denial of service (DOS) attack can make it impossible for legitimate users to access computer systems, network, services or other resources.

Title: CWE-400,664 - Resource leak
Description: Allocated resources are not released properly. This can slow down or crash your system. They must be closed along all paths to prevent a resource leak.
Remediation: **Problem**This line of code contains a resource that might not be closed properly. This can cause a resource leak that slows down or crashes your system.**Fix**Consider closing the following resource: *con*. The resource is allocated by call *sqlite3.connect*. The resource is referenced at lines 38, 39, 40. The resource is closed at line 40. There are other execution paths that do not contain closure statements, for example, when *Connection.commit()* throws an exception. To prevent this resource leak, close *con* in a try-finally block or declare it using a `with` statement.**More info**[View details about the `with` statement in the Python developer's guide](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0343/) (external link).

Title: CWE-327,328 - Weak algorithm used for Password Hashing
Description: Weak algorithm used for Password Hashing. Consider using stronger algorithms, such as Argon2, PBKDF2, or scrypt.
Remediation: To improve your code's security, use a password hashing function, such as the key derivation and stretching algorithm, the [PHC](https://password-hashing.net/) or [Argon2](https://password-hashing.net/#argon2), with the correct time and memory cost parameters. Avoid using the less secure `BLAKE2`, `MD5`, `SHA-1`, `SHA-256` and `SHA-3` hash functions for user passwords. Detector Name: Weak algorithm used for Password Hashing

Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule
Description: Resources that implement AutoCloseable or require explicit closure must be properly managed throughout their lifecycle. The recommended approach is to use try-with-resources block which ensures automatic and safe resource closure, regardless of whether an exception occurs or not. Failing to properly close resources can lead to resource leaks, memory issues, and system instability. This applies to both application resources and testing components.
Remediation: The code is not properly closing resources, which can lead to resource leaks. Unclosed resources may cause memory leaks or other system resource issues, potentially degrading application performance over time. If an exception occurs before the explicit close() call, the resource will not be closed, increasing the risk of leaks. To remediate this, use the try-with-resources statement to automatically close AutoCloseable resources.Alternatively, if try-with-resources cannot be used, ensure that close() is called in a finally block to guarantee proper resource cleanup even when exceptions occur.[Learn more](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/essential/exceptions/tryResourceClose.html)

The lack of authentication checks can cause the exposure of information, denial of service, and arbitrary code execution. We recommend that you apply authentication checks to all access points.
Remediation: This route is missing an authentication check, which may allow unauthorized access to sensitive functionality. Ensure that authentication middleware or mechanisms are applied to protect critical routes. Failing to do so can lead to security vulnerabilities such as unauthorized access. Learn more: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Authentication_Cheat_Sheet.html

Title: CWE-352,1275 - Cross-site request forgery
Description: Insecure configuration can lead to a cross-site request forgery (CSRF) vulnerability. This can enable an attacker to trick end users into performing unwanted actions while authenticated.
Remediation: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) protection is missing for state-changing operations. This vulnerability allows attackers to trick authenticated users into unknowingly performing unintended actions (e.g., changing passwords, transferring funds, or modifying data) by submitting malicious requests on their behalf. Implement anti-CSRF tokens for all POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE operations, or use SameSite cookies with 'strict' or 'lax' settings as an alternative defense. Reference: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Cross-Site_Request_Forgery_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html

Title: CWE-798 - Hardcoded credentials
Description: Hardcoded credentials can be intercepted by malicious actors. Even after removing them from the code they may still pose a risk because an attacker might have recorded them to use them at a later point in time.
Remediation: We detected a hardcoded database connection password in your code. This could allow anyone with access to your server to perform privileged actions on the database. We recommend that you avoid hardcoded passwords when connecting to a database and instead use environment variables or a configuration file to set the password.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/259.html)

Title: CWE-327,328,326,208,1240 - Insecure cryptography
Description: Misuse of cryptography-related APIs can create security vulnerabilities. This includes one or more of the following: algorithms with known weaknesses, certain padding modes, lack of integrity checks, and insufficiently large key sizes.
Remediation: Your code uses a non-standard or weak password encoder, which could allow an attacker to gain access to protected data. To protect your code, use a standard and secure encoder like `BCryptPasswordEncoder`, `Pbkdf2PasswordEncoder`, `Argon2PasswordEncoder`, or `SCryptPasswordEncoder`. Avoid the use of the following encoders: `ShaPasswordEncoder`, `Md5PasswordEncoder`, `LdapShaPasswordEncoder`, `Md4PasswordEncoder`, `MessageDigestPasswordEncoder`, `NoOpPasswordEncoder`, and `StandardPasswordEncoder`.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/328.html)

Description: Insecure configuration can lead to a cross-site request forgery (CSRF) vulnerability. This can enable an attacker to trick end users into performing unwanted actions while authenticated.
Remediation: Disabling cross-site request forgery (CSRF) protection can make your users vulnerable to attacks. Enable CSRF if your application can be accessed through a browser and another token mechanism isn't in place.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/352.html)

Title: CWE-20,79,80 - Cross-site scripting
Description: User-controllable input must be sanitized before it's included in output used to dynamically generate a web page. Unsanitized user input can introduce cross-side scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities that can lead to inadvertedly running malicious code in a trusted context.
Remediation: Untrusted data must be properly encoded or sanitized before beingincorporated into web page content or used to generate dynamic output.Failure to do so can result in Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities,enabling attackers to inject malicious scripts into the application. Thesescripts, when executed in users' browsers, can lead to various securitybreaches such as session hijacking, data theft, or unauthorized actionsperformed within the victim's session context. To mitigate this risk,use built-in template escaping mechanisms, HTML escape functions like`html.escape()`, or dedicated security libraries such as Bleach.Learn more: https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/xss/

Title: CWE-94 - Unsanitized input is run as code
Description: Running scripts generated from unsanitized inputs (for example, evaluating expressions that include user-provided strings) can lead to malicious behavior and inadvertently running code remotely.
Remediation: Untrusted input is passed to a method that executes arbitrary code, creating a code injection vulnerability. This allows attackers to execute malicious code within the application, potentially leading to data theft or system compromise. Validate input using allowlists with `Arrays.asList().contains()`, use regex matching with `String.matches()`, or parse input safely with `Integer.parseInt()` or `Boolean.parseBoolean()`. [Learn more](https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/Code_Injection)
Detector Name: Unsanitized input is run as code

Title: CWE-117,93 - Log injection
Description: User-provided inputs must be sanitized before they are logged. An attacker can use unsanitized input to break a log's integrity, forge log entries, or bypass log monitors.
Remediation: Potential log injection detected. Ensure all untrusted input is properly sanitized before logging. Use parameterized logging or validate input against a allow list to prevent log injection vulnerabilities. Consider using a dedicated logging library's built-in sanitization features when available. Learn more - https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/117.html

Title: CWE-502 - Deserialization of untrusted object
Description: Deserialization of untrusted or potentially malformed data can be exploited for denial of service or to induce running untrusted code.
Remediation: It looks like you are constructing an unsafe instance of `Yaml`, which might allow attackers to execute arbitrary code on the server, abuse application logic or lead to denial of service attacks. We recommend you to use a `SafeConstructor` when constructing a new `Yaml`.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/502.html)

Title: CWE-22,23 - Path traversal
Description: Creating file paths from untrusted input could allow a malicious actor to access arbitrary files on a disk by manipulating the file name in the path.
Remediation: Path traversal vulnerability detected. An attacker could access files outside the intended directory, potentially exposing sensitive information or executing malicious code. Validate and sanitize file paths by using path.normalize() and checking that the resolved path is within the expected directory. Learn More - https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/22.html.

Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule
Description: Resources that implement AutoCloseable or require explicit closure must be properly managed throughout their lifecycle. The recommended approach is to use try-with-resources block which ensures automatic and safe resource closure, regardless of whether an exception occurs or not. Failing to properly close resources can lead to resource leaks, memory issues, and system instability. This applies to both application resources and testing components.
Remediation: The code is not properly closing resources, which can lead to resource leaks. Unclosed resources may cause memory leaks or other system resource issues, potentially degrading application performance over time. If an exception occurs before the explicit close() call, the resource will not be closed, increasing the risk of leaks. To remediate this, use the try-with-resources statement to automatically close AutoCloseable resources.Alternatively, if try-with-resources cannot be used, ensure that close() is called in a finally block to guarantee proper resource cleanup even when exceptions occur.[Learn more](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/essential/exceptions/tryResourceClose.html)

Title: CWE-188 - Zip Streaming
Description: Parse ZIP files using streaming, therefore using local headers instead of central headers are required by the ZIP specification.
Remediation: It appears that you are performing a read operation on a ZIP file by using streaming. This approach reads the local header entries in a ZIP file instead of the central directory as required by the ZIP file specification. This could enable an attacker to set different names, permissions and substitute content on or bypass validation controls that use non-streaming approaches.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/188.html)

Title: CWE-22,23 - Path traversal
Description: Creating file paths from untrusted input might give a malicious actor access to sensitive files by manipulating the file name to access an arbitrary file on disk.
Remediation: Path traversal vulnerability detected. User-controlled input in file pathscan allow attackers to access files outside intended directories using`../` sequences. Secure your code by using framework functions like`getCanonicalPath()`, `normalize()`, or validating paths with`.startsWith()` checks. Learn more:https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/22.html

Title: CWE-77,78,88 - OS command injection
Description: Constructing operating system or shell commands with unsanitized user input can lead to inadvertently running malicious code.
Remediation: Incorporating user-supplied input into Python subprocess, OS, and command functions without proper validation or sanitization exposes your application to command injection vulnerabilities. This can enable malicious actors to execute arbitrary OS commands. To mitigate this risk, implement proper input sanitization using functions like `shlex.quote()` to ensure safe handling of user-supplied data. [Learn more](https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/OS_Command_Injection_Defense_Cheat_Sheet.html)

Title: CWE-73 - Sendfile injection
Description: User-provided inputs must be sanitized before being passed to `res.sendFile`. Otherwise an attacker could arbitrarily read files on the system through path traversal.

Title: CWE-400 - Denial of service rule
Description: A denial of service (DOS) attack can make it impossible for legitimate users to access computer systems, network, services or other resources.
Remediation: An attacker can provide an input stream or file that contains an excessive number of lines without a terminating condition. The call to `readLine()` might allow an attacker to crash the program or otherwise make it unavailable to legitimate users which causes denial of service. To avoid this, make sure you either enforce a limit on the lines read or apply proper input sanitization.

Title: CWE-329,1204,323 - Weak Initialization Vector (IV)
Description: Using a static or predictable initialization vector (IV) for a cryptographic cipher can make the resulting ciphertext much more predictable and susceptible to dictionary attacks.
Remediation: Using insecure random number generators can lead to predictable random values. This predictability can be exploited by attackers to guess sensitive values or break cryptographic operations. To fix this, use `new SecureRandom()` to instantiate a `java.security.SecureRandom` object instead of using non-cryptographic random number generators (like java.util.Random, Math.random(), or Apache Commons random utilities). For more information about secure random number generation, see: https://owasp.org/www-community/vulnerabilities/Insecure_Randomness

Remediation: It appears that you are using the `unserialize()` or `load` function for `js-yaml` on tainted input. This happens when users directly deserialize unvalidated malicious or hostile data that attackers send to the in-memory structure. This unvalidated data could lead to remote code execution, data compromise, ransomware attacks, access control attacks, denial of service (DoS) attacks, server crashes, authentication bypass, SQL injections, or path traversal.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/502.html)

Title: CWE-807 - Untrusted data in security decision
Description: Security decisions should not depend on branching. Because branching can be influenced by untrusted or client-provided data. For example, using a client-provided session ID (instead of a server-provided ID) in a conditional statement might allow an attacker to search for IDs of active sessions.
Remediation: We detected that untrusted/user data is being used for decision making. Make sure the data is properly sanitised, else it could expose the sensitive information to the attacker. [Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/807.html)

Title: CWE-400,664 - Resource leak
Description: Allocated resources are not released properly. This can slow down or crash your system. They must be closed along all paths to prevent a resource leak.
Remediation: **Problem**This line of code contains a resource that might not be closed properly. This can cause a resource leak that slows down or crashes your system.**Fix**Consider closing the following resource: *conn*. The resource is allocated by call *sqlite3.connect*. The resource is referenced at lines 18, 21. The resource is closed at line 21. There are other execution paths that do not contain closure statements, for example, when *Connection.cursor()* throws an exception. To prevent this resource leak, close *conn* in a try-finally block or declare it using a `with` statement.**More info**[View details about the `with` statement in the Python developer's guide](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0343/) (external link).

Title: CWE-489 - Enabling and overriding debug feature
Description: Don't enable or override an application's debug feature. Instead, use OS environment variables to set up the debug feature.
Remediation: Enabling an application's debug or test feature makes it easier for developers to find bugs, but it also gives attackers access to detailed information about both the system running the application and users. To make your code secure, do not enable the debug or test feature on production servers. For more information, see [CWE-489](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/489.html).

Title: CWE-208 - Timing attack
Description: Operators like `==` and `===` are not time-safe and can make your application vulnerable to a timing attack, which might enable attackers to infer security-sensitive information.
Remediation: Avoid using `==` and `===` operators with sensitive data fields like passwords, api keys, tokens etc. as they are vulnerable to timing attack. Consider using `crypto.timingSafeEqual` or `secure-compare` for time-safe string comparison.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/208.html)

Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule
Description: Resources that implement AutoCloseable or require explicit closure must be properly managed throughout their lifecycle. Failing to properly close resources can lead to resource leaks, memory issues, and system instability. This applies to both application resources and testing components.
Remediation: The code is not properly closing resources, which can lead to resource leaks. Unclosed resources may cause memory leaks or other system resource issues, potentially degrading application performance over time. If an exception occurs before the explicit close() call, the resource will not be closed, increasing the risk of leaks. To remediate this, use the try-with-resources statement to automatically close AutoCloseable resources.Alternatively, if try-with-resources cannot be used, ensure that close() is called in a finally block to guarantee proper resource cleanup even when exceptions occur.[Learn more](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/essential/exceptions/tryResourceClose.html)Similar issue at line number 14.

Title: CWE-400,664 - Resource leak
Description: Allocated resources are not released properly. This can slow down or crash your system. They must be closed along all paths to prevent a resource leak.
Remediation: **Problem**This line of code contains a resource that might not be closed properly. This can cause a resource leak that slows down or crashes your system.**Fix**Consider closing the following resource: *conn*. The resource is allocated by call *sqlite3.connect*. The resource is referenced at lines 38, 41. The resource is closed at line 41. There are other execution paths that do not contain closure statements, for example, when *Connection.cursor()* throws an exception. To prevent this resource leak, close *conn* in a try-finally block or declare it using a `with` statement.**More info**[View details about the `with` statement in the Python developer's guide](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0343/) (external link).

Title: CWE-352,1275 - Cross-site request forgery
Description: Insecure configuration can lead to a cross-site request forgery (CSRF) vulnerability. This can enable an attacker to trick end users into performing unwanted actions while authenticated.
Remediation: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) protection is missing for state-changing operations. This vulnerability allows attackers to trick authenticated users into unknowingly performing unintended actions (e.g., changing passwords, transferring funds, or modifying data) by submitting malicious requests on their behalf. Implement anti-CSRF tokens for all POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE operations, or use SameSite cookies with 'strict' or 'lax' settings as an alternative defense. Reference: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Cross-Site_Request_Forgery_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html

Title: CWE-329,1204,323 - Weak Initialization Vector (IV)
Description: Using a static or predictable initialization vector (IV) for a cryptographic cipher can make the resulting ciphertext much more predictable and susceptible to dictionary attacks.
Remediation: Using insecure random number generators can lead to predictable random values. This predictability can be exploited by attackers to guess sensitive values or break cryptographic operations. To fix this, use `new SecureRandom()` to instantiate a `java.security.SecureRandom` object instead of using non-cryptographic random number generators (like java.util.Random, Math.random(), or Apache Commons random utilities). For more information about secure random number generation, see: https://owasp.org/www-community/vulnerabilities/Insecure_Randomness

Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule

Title: CWE-798 - Hardcoded credentials

Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule
Description: Resources that implement AutoCloseable or require explicit closure must be properly managed throughout their lifecycle. The recommended approach is to use try-with-resources block which ensures automatic and safe resource closure, regardless of whether an exception occurs or not. Failing to properly close resources can lead to resource leaks, memory issues, and system instability. This applies to both application resources and testing components.
Remediation: The code is not properly closing resources, which can lead to resource leaks. Unclosed resources may cause memory leaks or other system resource issues, potentially degrading application performance over time. If an exception occurs before the explicit close() call, the resource will not be closed, increasing the risk of leaks. To remediate this, use the try-with-resources statement to automatically close AutoCloseable resources.Alternatively, if try-with-resources cannot be used, ensure that close() is called in a finally block to guarantee proper resource cleanup even when exceptions occur.[Learn more](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/essential/exceptions/tryResourceClose.html)Similar issue at line numbers 11 and 16.

Title: CWE-352,1275 - Cross-site request forgery
Description: Insecure configuration can lead to a cross-site request forgery (CSRF) vulnerability. This can enable an attacker to trick end users into performing unwanted actions while authenticated.
Remediation: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) protection is missing for state-changing operations. This vulnerability allows attackers to trick authenticated users into unknowingly performing unintended actions (e.g., changing passwords, transferring funds, or modifying data) by submitting malicious requests on their behalf. Implement anti-CSRF tokens for all POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE operations, or use SameSite cookies with 'strict' or 'lax' settings as an alternative defense. Reference: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Cross-Site_Request_Forgery_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html

Title: CWE-20,79,80 - Cross-site scripting

Title: CWE-329,1204,323 - Weak Initialization Vector (IV)
Description: Using a static or predictable initialization vector (IV) for a cryptographic cipher can make the resulting ciphertext much more predictable and susceptible to dictionary attacks.
Remediation: Using insecure random number generators can lead to predictable random values. This predictability can be exploited by attackers to guess sensitive values or break cryptographic operations. To fix this, use `new SecureRandom()` to instantiate a `java.security.SecureRandom` object instead of using non-cryptographic random number generators (like java.util.Random, Math.random(), or Apache Commons random utilities). For more information about secure random number generation, see: https://owasp.org/www-community/vulnerabilities/Insecure_Randomness

Title: CWE-352,1275 - Cross-site request forgery
Description: Insecure configuration can lead to a cross-site request forgery (CSRF) vulnerability. This can enable an attacker to trick end users into performing unwanted actions while authenticated.
Remediation: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) protection is missing for state-changing operations. This vulnerability allows attackers to trick authenticated users into unknowingly performing unintended actions (e.g., changing passwords, transferring funds, or modifying data) by submitting malicious requests on their behalf. Implement anti-CSRF tokens for all POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE operations, or use SameSite cookies with 'strict' or 'lax' settings as an alternative defense. Reference: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Cross-Site_Request_Forgery_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html

Title: CWE-400,664 - Resource leak
Description: Allocated resources are not released properly. This can slow down or crash your system. They must be closed along all paths to prevent a resource leak.
Remediation: **Problem**This line of code contains a resource that might not be closed properly. This can cause a resource leak that slows down or crashes your system.**Fix**Consider closing the following resource: *conn*. The resource is allocated by call *sqlite3.connect*. The resource is referenced at lines 30, 33, 35. The resource is closed at lines 33, 35. There are other execution paths that do not contain closure statements, for example, when *Connection.cursor()* throws an exception. To prevent this resource leak, close *conn* in a try-finally block or declare it using a `with` statement.**More info**[View details about the `with` statement in the Python developer's guide](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0343/) (external link).

Title: CWE-352,1275 - Cross-site request forgery
Description: Insecure configuration can lead to a cross-site request forgery (CSRF) vulnerability. This can enable an attacker to trick end users into performing unwanted actions while authenticated.
Remediation: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) protection is missing for state-changing operations. This vulnerability allows attackers to trick authenticated users into unknowingly performing unintended actions (e.g., changing passwords, transferring funds, or modifying data) by submitting malicious requests on their behalf. Implement anti-CSRF tokens for all POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE operations, or use SameSite cookies with 'strict' or 'lax' settings as an alternative defense. Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule
Description: Resources that implement AutoCloseable or require explicit closure must be properly managed throughout their lifecycle. The recommended approach is to use try-with-resources block which ensures automatic and safe resource closure, regardless of whether an exception occurs or not. Failing to properly close resources can lead to resource leaks, memory issues, and system instability. This applies to both application resources and testing components.
Remediation: The code is not properly closing resources, which can lead to resource leaks. Unclosed resources may cause memory leaks or other system resource issues, potentially degrading application performance over time. If an exception occurs before the explicit close() call, the resource will not be closed, increasing the risk of leaks. To remediate this, use the try-with-resources statement to automatically close AutoCloseable resources.Alternatively, if try-with-resources cannot be used, ensure that close() is called in a finally block to guarantee proper resource cleanup even when exceptions occur.[Learn more](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/essential/exceptions/tryResourceClose.html)

Remediation: Potential log injection detected. Ensure all untrusted input is properly sanitized before logging. Use parameterized logging or validate input against a allow list to prevent log injection vulnerabilities. Consider using a dedicated logging library's built-in sanitization features when available. Learn more - https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/117.html

Title: CWE-400,664 - Resource leak
Description: Allocated resources are not released properly. This can slow down or crash your system. They must be closed along all paths to prevent a resource leak.
Remediation: **Problem**This line of code contains a resource that might not be closed properly. This can cause a resource leak that slows down or crashes your system.**Fix**Consider closing the following resource: *cursor*. The resource is allocated by call *Connection.cursor*. The resource is referenced at lines 11, 12, 13, 14, 15. The resource is closed at line 15. There are other execution paths that do not contain closure statements, for example, when *Cursor.execute()* throws an exception. To prevent this resource leak, close *cursor* in a try-finally block or declare it using a `with` statement.**More info**[View details about the `with` statement in the Python developer's guide](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0343/) (external link).

Title: CWE-22 - Path traversal
Description: Constructing path names with unsanitized user input can lead to path traversal attacks (for example, `../../..`) that allow an attacker access to file system resources.
Remediation: Path traversal vulnerability detected. User-controlled input in file paths can allow attackers to access files outside intended directories using `../` sequences. Secure your code by using framework functions like `safe_join()`, `secure_filename()` and `.startswith()` checks. Learn more: https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/22.html

Title: CWE-94 - Unsanitized input is run as code
Description: Running scripts generated from unsanitized inputs (for example, evaluating expressions that include user-provided strings) can lead to malicious behavior and inadvertently running code remotely.
Remediation: Untrusted input is passed to a method that executes arbitrary code, creating a code injection vulnerability. This allows attackers to execute malicious code within the application, potentially leading to data theft or system compromise. Validate input using allowlists with `Arrays.asList().contains()`, use regex matching with `String.matches()`, or parse input safely with `Integer.parseInt()` or `Boolean.parseBoolean()`. [Learn more](https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/Code_Injection)
Detector Name: Unsanitized input is run as code

Title: CWE-798 - Hardcoded credentials
Description: Hardcoded credentials can be intercepted by malicious actors. Even after removing them from the code they may still pose a risk because an attacker might have recorded them to use them at a later point in time.
Remediation: It appears your code contains a hardcoded password. This poses a serious security risk as it may allow unauthorized access to sensitive data or systems. To remediate this issue, it is recommended to use AWS Secrets Manager to store and access secrets:	a) Visit Secrets Manager on the AWS Management Console.	b) Choose the secret type on the console page and follow the instructions.	c) Use the suggested code samples to retrieve the secret.To learn more about password management best practices, see [OWASP password storage cheat sheet](https://www.owasp.org/index.php/Password_Storage_Cheat_Sheet).

Title: Do not fetch sensitive information from a GET request.

Title: CWE-489 - Enabling and overriding debug feature
Description: Don't enable or override an application's debug feature. Instead, use OS environment variables to set up the debug feature.
Remediation: Enabling an application's debug or test feature makes it easier for developers to find bugs, but it also gives attackers access to detailed information about both the system running the application and users. To make your code secure, do not enable the debug or test feature on production servers. For more information, see [CWE-489](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/489.html).

Title: CWE-489 - Enabling and overriding debug feature
Description: Don't enable or override an application's debug feature. Instead, use OS environment variables to set up the debug feature.
Remediation: Don't hardcode the `DEBUG` or `SECRET_KEY` variable because some system extensions might be configured to use a different value. Instead, use OS environment variables to set up the debug feature.

Title: CWE-22,23 - Path traversal
Description: Creating file paths from untrusted input could allow a malicious actor to access arbitrary files on a disk by manipulating the file name in the path.
Remediation: Path traversal vulnerability detected. An attacker could access files outside the intended directory, potentially exposing sensitive information or executing malicious code. Validate and sanitize file paths by using path.normalize() and checking that the resolved path is within the expected directory. Learn More - https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/22.html.

Title: CWE-208 - Timing attack
Description: Operators like `==` and `===` are not time-safe and can make your application vulnerable to a timing attack, which might enable attackers to infer security-sensitive information.
Remediation: Avoid using `==` and `===` operators with sensitive data fields like passwords, api keys, tokens etc. as they are vulnerable to timing attack. Consider using `crypto.timingSafeEqual` or `secure-compare` for time-safe string comparison.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/208.html)

Title: CWE-770,400 - Limit request length

We recommend that you set the `fileSize` <= 8mb. [Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/400.html)

Title: CWE-78,77 - OS command injection
Description: Constructing operating system or shell commands with unsanitized user input can lead to inadvertently running malicious code.
Remediation: OS command injection vulnerability detected. User-controlled data is being used to construct a command that is executed by the system. Title: CWE-352,1275 - Cross-site request forgery
Description: Insecure configuration can lead to a cross-site request forgery (CSRF) vulnerability. This can enable an attacker to trick end users into performing unwanted actions while authenticated.
Remediation: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) protection is missing for state-changing operations. This vulnerability allows attackers to trick authenticated users into unknowingly performing unintended actions (e.g., changing passwords, transferring funds, or modifying data) by submitting malicious requests on their behalf. Implement anti-CSRF tokens for all POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE operations, or use SameSite cookies with 'strict' or 'lax' settings as an alternative defense. Reference: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Cross-Site_Request_Forgery_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html

Title: CWE-614 - Insecure cookie

Remediation: It appears that you are using an HTTP cookie without setting its `domain` or `path` attribute. Title: CWE-400 - Denial of service rule
Description: A denial of service (DOS) attack can make it impossible for legitimate users to access computer systems, network, services or other resources.
Remediation: An attacker can provide an input stream or file that contains an excessive number of lines without a terminating condition. The call to `readLine()` might allow an attacker to crash the program or otherwise make it unavailable to legitimate users which causes denial of service. To avoid this, make sure you either enforce a limit on the lines read or apply proper input sanitization.

Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule
Description: Resources that implement AutoCloseable or require explicit closure must be properly managed throughout their lifecycle. The recommended approach is to use try-with-resources block which ensures automatic and safe resource closure, regardless of whether an exception occurs or not. Failing to properly close resources can lead to resource leaks, memory issues, and system instability. This applies to both application resources and testing components.
Remediation: The code is not properly closing resources, which can lead to resource leaks. Unclosed resources may cause memory leaks or other system resource issues, potentially degrading application performance over time. If an exception occurs before the explicit close() call, the resource will not be closed, increasing the risk of leaks. To remediate this, use the try-with-resources statement to automatically close AutoCloseable resources.Alternatively, if try-with-resources cannot be used, ensure that close() is called in a finally block to guarantee proper resource cleanup even when exceptions occur.[Learn more](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/essential/exceptions/tryResourceClose.html)

Title: CWE-329,1204,323 - Weak Initialization Vector (IV)
Description: Using a static or predictable initialization vector (IV) for a cryptographic cipher can make the resulting ciphertext much more predictable and susceptible to dictionary attacks.
Remediation: Using insecure random number generators can lead to predictable random values. This predictability can be exploited by attackers to guess sensitive values or break cryptographic operations. To fix this, use `new SecureRandom()` to instantiate a `java.security.SecureRandom` object instead of using non-cryptographic random number generators (like java.util.Random, Math.random(), or Apache Commons random utilities). For more information about secure random number generation, see: https://owasp.org/www-community/vulnerabilities/Insecure_Randomness

Title: CWE-352,1275 - Cross-site request forgery
Description: Insecure configuration can lead to a cross-site request forgery (CSRF) vulnerability. This can enable an attacker to trick end users into performing unwanted actions while authenticated.
Remediation: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) protection is missing for state-changing operations. This vulnerability allows attackers to trick authenticated users into unknowingly performing unintended actions (e.g., changing passwords, transferring funds, or modifying data) by submitting malicious requests on their behalf. Implement anti-CSRF tokens for all POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE operations, or use SameSite cookies with 'strict' or 'lax' settings as an alternative defense. Reference: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Cross-Site_Request_Forgery_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html

Title: CWE-22,23 - Path traversal

Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule
Description: Resources that implement AutoCloseable or require explicit closure must be properly managed throughout their lifecycle. The recommended approach is to use try-with-resources block which ensures automatic and safe resource closure, regardless of whether an exception occurs or not. Failing to properly close resources can lead to resource leaks, memory issues, and system instability. This applies to both application resources and testing components.
Remediation: The code is not properly closing resources, which can lead to resource leaks. Unclosed resources may cause memory leaks or other system resource issues, potentially degrading application performance over time. If an exception occurs before the explicit close() call, the resource will not be closed, increasing the risk of leaks. To remediate this, use the try-with-resources statement to automatically close AutoCloseable resources.Alternatively, if try-with-resources cannot be used, ensure that close() is called in a finally block to guarantee proper resource cleanup even when exceptions occur.[Learn more](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/essential/exceptions/tryResourceClose.html)

Title: CWE-400,664 - Resource leak

This can cause a resource leak that slows down or crashes your system.**Fix**Consider closing the following resource: *con*. The resource is allocated by call *sqlite3.connect*. The resource is referenced at lines 23, 24. The resource is closed at line 24. There are other execution paths that do not contain closure statements, for example, when *Connection.commit()* throws an exception. To prevent this resource leak, close *con* in a try-finally block or declare it using a `with` statement.**More info**[View details about the `with` statement in the Python developer's guide](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0343/) (external link).

This vulnerability allows attackers to trick authenticated users into unknowingly performing unintended actions (e.g., changing passwords, transferring funds, or modifying data) by submitting malicious requests on their behalf. Implement anti-CSRF tokens for all POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE operations, or use SameSite cookies with 'strict' or 'lax' settings as an alternative defense. Title: CWE-22,23 - Path traversal
Description: Creating file paths from untrusted input could allow a malicious actor to access arbitrary files on a disk by manipulating the file name in the path.
Remediation: It appears that you are not sanitizing the file names of the entries in a .zip file archive. They should be considered untrusted and tainted, and should be validated before being used for file system operations. A successful attack might give an attacker the ability to read, modify, or delete sensitive information from the file system and sometimes even execute arbitrary operating system commands. We recommend that you sanitize the file names in .zip files before you extract them.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/20)

Title: CWE-400,664 - Resource leak
Description: Allocated resources are not released properly. This can slow down or crash your system. They must be closed along all paths to prevent a resource leak.
Remediation: **Problem**This line of code contains a resource that might not be closed properly. This can cause a resource leak that slows down or crashes your system.**Fix**Consider closing the following resource: *cursor*. The resource is allocated by call *Connection.cursor*. The resource is referenced at lines 19, 20, 28, 29. The resource is closed at line 29. There are other execution paths that do not contain closure statements, for example, when *Cursor.execute()* throws an exception. To prevent this resource leak, close *cursor* in a try-finally block or declare it using a `with` statement.**More info**[View details about the `with` statement in the Python developer's guide](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0343/) (external link).

Title: CWE-117,93 - Log injection

Title: CWE-798 - Hardcoded credentials
Description: Hardcoded credentials can be intercepted by malicious actors. Even after removing them from the code they may still pose a risk because an attacker might have recorded them to use them at a later point in time.
Remediation: It appears your code contains a hardcoded password. This poses a serious security risk as it may allow unauthorized access to sensitive data or systems. To remediate this issue, it is recommended to use AWS Secrets Manager to store and access secrets:	a) Visit Secrets Manager on the AWS Management Console.	b) Choose the secret type on the console page and follow the instructions.	c) Use the suggested code samples to retrieve the secret.To learn more about password management best practices, see [OWASP password storage cheat sheet](https://www.owasp.org/index.php/Password_Storage_Cheat_Sheet).

Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule
Description: Resources that implement AutoCloseable or require explicit closure must be properly managed throughout their lifecycle. The recommended approach is to use try-with-resources block which ensures automatic and safe resource closure, regardless of whether an exception occurs or not. Failing to properly close resources can lead to resource leaks, memory issues, and system instability. This applies to both application resources and testing components.
Remediation: The code is not properly closing resources, which can lead to resource leaks. Unclosed resources may cause memory leaks or other system resource issues, potentially degrading application performance over time. If an exception occurs before the explicit close() call, the resource will not be closed, increasing the risk of leaks. To remediate this, use the try-with-resources statement to automatically close AutoCloseable resources.Alternatively, if try-with-resources cannot be used, ensure that close() is called in a finally block to guarantee proper resource cleanup even when exceptions occur.[Learn more](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/essential/exceptions/tryResourceClose.html)

Title: CWE-329,1204,323 - Weak Initialization Vector (IV)
Description: Using a static or predictable initialization vector (IV) for a cryptographic cipher can make the resulting ciphertext much more predictable and susceptible to dictionary attacks.
Remediation: Using insecure random number generators can lead to predictable random values. This predictability can be exploited by attackers to guess sensitive values or break cryptographic operations. To fix this, use `new SecureRandom()` to instantiate a `java.security.SecureRandom` object instead of using non-cryptographic random number generators (like java.util.Random, Math.random(), or Apache Commons random utilities). For more information about secure random number generation, see: https://owasp.org/www-community/vulnerabilities/Insecure_Randomness

Title: CWE-22,23 - Path traversal

Title: CWE-400,664 - Resource leak

Title: CWE-400 - Denial of service rule

Title: CWE-329,1204,323 - Weak Initialization Vector (IV)
Description: Using a static or predictable initialization vector (IV) for a cryptographic cipher can make the resulting ciphertext much more predictable and susceptible to dictionary attacks.
Remediation: Using insecure random number generators can lead to predictable random values. This predictability can be exploited by attackers to guess sensitive values or break cryptographic operations. To fix this, use `new SecureRandom()` to instantiate a `java.security.SecureRandom` object instead of using non-cryptographic random number generators (like java.util.Random, Math.random(), or Apache Commons random utilities). For more information about secure random number generation, see: https://owasp.org/www-community/vulnerabilities/Insecure_Randomness

Title: CWE-79,80 - Cross-site scripting
Description: User-controllable input must be sanitized before it's included in output used to dynamically generate a web page. Unsanitized user input can introduce cross-side scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities that can lead to inadvertedly running malicious code in a trusted context.
Remediation: A potential Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability has been identified, where untrusted input is being reflected in the response without proper sanitization. This can allow an attacker to inject and execute malicious scripts in the user's browser, potentially compromising sensitive data or application behavior. To mitigate this risk, it is strongly recommended to sanitize or encode all user input before rendering it in the DOM or returning it in a response. Title: CWE-352,1275 - Cross-site request forgery
Description: Insecure configuration can lead to a cross-site request forgery (CSRF) vulnerability. This can enable an attacker to trick end users into performing unwanted actions while authenticated.
Remediation: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) protection is missing for state-changing operations. This vulnerability allows attackers to trick authenticated users into unknowingly performing unintended actions (e.g., changing passwords, transferring funds, or modifying data) by submitting malicious requests on their behalf. Implement anti-CSRF tokens for all POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE operations, or use SameSite cookies with 'strict' or 'lax' settings as an alternative defense. Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule
Description: Resources that implement AutoCloseable or require explicit closure must be properly managed throughout their lifecycle. The recommended approach is to use try-with-resources block which ensures automatic and safe resource closure, regardless of whether an exception occurs or not. Failing to properly close resources can lead to resource leaks, memory issues, and system instability. This applies to both application resources and testing components.
Remediation: The code is not properly closing resources, which can lead to resource leaks. Unclosed resources may cause memory leaks or other system resource issues, potentially degrading application performance over time. If an exception occurs before the explicit close() call, the resource will not be closed, increasing the risk of leaks. To remediate this, use the try-with-resources statement to automatically close AutoCloseable resources.Alternatively, if try-with-resources cannot be used, ensure that close() is called in a finally block to guarantee proper resource cleanup even when exceptions occur.[Learn more](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/essential/exceptions/tryResourceClose.html)Similar issue at line number 27.

Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule
Description: Resources that implement AutoCloseable or require explicit closure must be properly managed throughout their lifecycle. The recommended approach is to use try-with-resources block which ensures automatic and safe resource closure, regardless of whether an exception occurs or not. Failing to properly close resources can lead to resource leaks, memory issues, and system instability. This applies to both application resources and testing components.
Remediation: The code is not properly closing resources, which can lead to resource leaks. Unclosed resources may cause memory leaks or other system resource issues, potentially degrading application performance over time. If an exception occurs before the explicit close() call, the resource will not be closed, increasing the risk of leaks. To remediate this, use the try-with-resources statement to automatically close AutoCloseable resources.Alternatively, if try-with-resources cannot be used, ensure that close() is called in a finally block to guarantee proper resource cleanup even when exceptions occur.[Learn more](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/essential/exceptions/tryResourceClose.html)

Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule

Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule

Title: CWE-404,772,459 - Missing release of resources rule
Description: Resources that implement AutoCloseable or require explicit closure must be properly managed throughout their lifecycle. The recommended approach is to use try-with-resources block which ensures automatic and safe resource closure, regardless of whether an exception occurs or not. Failing to properly close resources can lead to resource leaks, memory issues, and system instability. This applies to both application resources and testing components.
Remediation: The code is not properly closing resources, which can lead to resource leaks. Unclosed resources may cause memory leaks or other system resource issues, potentially degrading application performance over time. If an exception occurs before the explicit close() call, the resource will not be closed, increasing the risk of leaks. To remediate this, use the try-with-resources statement to automatically close AutoCloseable resources.Alternatively, if try-with-resources cannot be used, ensure that close() is called in a finally block to guarantee proper resource cleanup even when exceptions occur.[Learn more](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/essential/exceptions/tryResourceClose.html)

Remediation: Disabling cross-site request forgery (CSRF) protection can make your users vulnerable to attacks. Enable CSRF if your application can be accessed through a browser and another token mechanism isn't in place.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/352.html)

Title: CWE-117,93 - Log injection
Description: User-provided inputs must be sanitized before they are logged. An attacker can use unsanitized input to break a log's integrity, forge log entries, or bypass log monitors.
Remediation: Potential log injection detected. Ensure all untrusted input is properly sanitized before logging. Use parameterized logging or validate input against a allow list to prevent log injection vulnerabilities. Consider using a dedicated logging library's built-in sanitization features when available. Learn more - https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/117.html

Title: CWE-798 - Hardcoded credentials
Description: Hardcoded credentials can be intercepted by malicious actors. Even after removing them from the code they may still pose a risk because an attacker might have recorded them to use them at a later point in time.
Remediation: It appears your code contains a hardcoded password. This poses a serious security risk as it may allow unauthorized access to sensitive data or systems. To remediate this issue, it is recommended to use AWS Secrets Manager to store and access secrets:	a) Visit Secrets Manager on the AWS Management Console.	b) Choose the secret type on the console page and follow the instructions.	c) Use the suggested code samples to retrieve the secret.To learn more about password management best practices, see [OWASP password storage cheat sheet](https://www.owasp.org/index.php/Password_Storage_Cheat_Sheet).

Title: CWE-400 - Denial of service rule
Description: A denial of service (DOS) attack can make it impossible for legitimate users to access computer systems, network, services or other resources.
Remediation: An attacker can provide an input stream or file that contains an excessive number of lines without a terminating condition. The call to `readLine()` might allow an attacker to crash the program or otherwise make it unavailable to legitimate users which causes denial of service. To avoid this, make sure you either enforce a limit on the lines read or apply proper input sanitization.

Title: CWE-352,1275 - Cross-site request forgery
Description: Insecure configuration can lead to a cross-site request forgery (CSRF) vulnerability. This can enable an attacker to trick end users into performing unwanted actions while authenticated.
Remediation: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) protection is missing for state-changing operations. This vulnerability allows attackers to trick authenticated users into unknowingly performing unintended actions (e.g., changing passwords, transferring funds, or modifying data) by submitting malicious requests on their behalf. Implement anti-CSRF tokens for all POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE operations, or use SameSite cookies with 'strict' or 'lax' settings as an alternative defense. Reference: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Cross-Site_Request_Forgery_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html

Title: CWE-489 - Enabling and overriding debug feature
Description: Don't enable or override an application's debug feature. Instead, use OS environment variables to set up the debug feature.
Remediation: Don't hardcode the `DEBUG` or `SECRET_KEY` variable because some system extensions might be configured to use a different value. Instead, use OS environment variables to set up the debug feature.

Title: CWE-327,328,326,208,1240 - Insecure cryptography
Description: Misuse of cryptography-related APIs can create security vulnerabilities. This includes one or more of the following: algorithms with known weaknesses, certain padding modes, lack of integrity checks, and insufficiently large key sizes.
Remediation: Hashing algorithms supported by `java.security.MessageDigest` are not suitable for encoding passwords. Consider using a standard and secure password encoder like `BCryptPasswordEncoder`, `Pbkdf2PasswordEncoder`, `Argon2PasswordEncoder`, or `SCryptPasswordEncoder`. For more information, see [CWE-328](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/328.html) and [CWE-916](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/916.html).

Title: CWE-22,23 - Path traversal
Description: Creating file paths from untrusted input could allow a malicious actor to access arbitrary files on a disk by manipulating the file name in the path.
Remediation: Path traversal vulnerability detected. An attacker could access files outside the intended directory, potentially exposing sensitive information or executing malicious code. Validate and sanitize file paths by using path.normalize() and checking that the resolved path is within the expected directory. Learn More - https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/22.html.

Title: CWE-94 - Unsanitized input is run as code
Description: Running scripts generated from unsanitized inputs (for example, evaluating expressions that include user-provided strings) can lead to malicious behavior and inadvertently running code remotely.
Remediation: The code is using unsanitized inputs to run scripts, which creates a remote code execution vulnerability, allowing attackers to inject and execute arbitrary code on your system. This can lead to complete system compromise, unauthorized access to sensitive data, or service disruption. To remediate this issue, implement input validation and sanitization using libraries like OWASP ESAPI or Apache Commons Lang's StringEscapeUtils. Additionally, consider using a content security policy and running scripts in a sandboxed environment with limited privileges. [Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/94.html)
Detector Name: Unsanitized input is run as code

Title: CWE-188 - Zip Streaming
Description: Parse ZIP files using streaming, therefore using local headers instead of central headers are required by the ZIP specification.
Remediation: It appears that you are performing a read operation on a ZIP file by using streaming. This approach reads the local header entries in a ZIP file instead of the central directory as required by the ZIP file specification. This could enable an attacker to set different names, permissions and substitute content on or bypass validation controls that use non-streaming approaches.[Learn more](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/188.html)
